entries.name: remark_1
entries.statement: We expect that the method developed here can be extended to the inhomogeneous setting with an additional one-body potential $V$, in the spirit of \cite{LewNamRou-21, CKRTG26}. In such a situation, the main high-frequency analysis should remain essentially the same, while the low-frequency comparison would have to be adapted to the presence of $V$. In particular, one should then be able to replace the Yukawa potential by the Coulomb potential. We do not pursue this extension here.

entries.name: rem:frac-bessel-implemented
thm:main-result
entries.statement:
[Main result]
	\label{thm:main-result}
	{#1{As $\lambda\downarrow0$, the following hold for the fixed fractional-Bessel interaction with $\frac32<\beta\le2$.}}
	\begin{enumerate}
		\item The relative free energy converges:
		\[
		-\log\frac{Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}{Z_0}
		\longrightarrow
		-\log z_{\mathrm{cl}}.
		\]
		\item For every fixed $k\ge1$,
		\[
		k!\lambda^k\Gamma_\lambda^{(k)}
		\longrightarrow
		\int |u^{\otimes k}\rangle\langle u^{\otimes k}|\,\,\mathrm d\nu(u)
		\qquad\text{in }\mathfrak S^2(\mathfrak{H}^{\otimes_s k}).
		\]
	\end{enumerate}
	%	In particular, for every $n\ge1$ and every $\phi\in L^2(\mathbb T^2)$ with finite Fourier support,
	%	\[
	%	n!\lambda^n\big\langle \phi^{\otimes n},\Gamma_\lambda^{(n)}\phi^{\otimes n}\big\rangle
	%	\longrightarrow
	%	\int |\langle \phi,u\rangle|^{2n}\,\,\mathrm d\nu(u).
	%	\]

entries.name: lem:relent-signedA
entries.proof:
Since
	\[
	\log\Gamma_s=-\lambda\, \mathrm d\Gamma(h-sA)-\log Z_s,
	\qquad
	\partial_s \log Z_s=\lambda\,\mathrm{Tr}(A\gamma_s),
	\]
	a direct computation yields the exact identity
	\begin{equation}
		\label{eq:relent-path}
		\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_0)
		=
		\lambda\int_0^1 \mathrm{Tr}\!\big(A(\gamma_1-\gamma_s)\big)\,\,\mathrm d .
	\end{equation}
	Fix $s\in[0,1)$. Set
	\[
	h_s:=h-sA,
	\qquad
	A_s:=(1-s)A,
	\]
	so that
	\[
	h_s-A_s=h-A,
	\qquad
	\gamma_s=(e^{\lambda h_s}-1)^{-1},
	\qquad
	\gamma_1=(e^{\lambda(h_s-A_s)}-1)^{-1}.
	\]
	Hence
	\begin{equation}
		\label{eq:integrand-rewrite}
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(A(\gamma_1-\gamma_s)\big)
		=
		\frac{1}{1-s}\,
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(A_s\Big(\frac{1}{e^{\lambda(h_s-A_s)}-1}-\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h_s}-1}\Big)\Big).
	\end{equation}
	From \eqref{eq:A-upper-bound} we have
	\[
	h_s=h-sA \ge (1-sc)h>0,
	\qquad
	A_s=(1-s)A \le (1-s)c\,h \le \frac{(1-s)c}{1-sc}\,h_s.
	\]
	Define
	\[
	c_s:=\frac{(1-s)c}{1-sc}\in(0,1),
	\qquad
	\text{so that } A_s\le c_s h_s.
	\]
	Applying \cite[Lemma 6.3]{LewNamRou-21}  to the pair $(\lambda h_s,\lambda A_s)$ gives
	\begin{equation}
		\label{eq:lemma63-applied}
		0\le
		\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(A_s\Big(\frac{1}{e^{\lambda(h_s-A_s)}-1}-\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h_s}-1}\Big)\Big)
		\le
		\frac{1}{1-c_s}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big).
	\end{equation}
	Combining \eqref{eq:integrand-rewrite} and \eqref{eq:lemma63-applied} yields, for $s\in[0,1)$,
	\begin{equation}
		\label{eq:integrand-bound}
		\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(A(\gamma_1-\gamma_s)\big)
		\le
		\frac{1}{1-s}\cdot \frac{1}{1-c_s}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big).
	\end{equation}
	First note that
	\[
	1-c_s = 1-\frac{(1-s)c}{1-sc}=\frac{1-c}{1-sc}
	\qquad\Longrightarrow\qquad
	\frac{1}{1-c_s}=\frac{1-sc}{1-c}.
	\]
	Next, since $h_s\ge (1-sc)h$, we have $h_s^{-1}\le (1-sc)^{-1}h^{-1}$ and hence
	\begin{align*}
	&\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big)
	=
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1/2}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_sh_s^{-1/2}\big)
	\\
	&\le \frac1{1-sc}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1/2}A_s\,h^{-1}A_sh_s^{-1/2}\big)
	=
	\frac1{1-sc}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1/2}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_sh^{-1/2}\big)
	\\
	&\le \frac1{(1-sc)^2}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1/2}A_s\,h^{-1}A_sh^{-1/2}\big)
	=
	\frac{(1-s)^2}{(1-sc)^2}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1}Ah^{-1}A\big).
	\end{align*}
	Insert these two relations into \eqref{eq:integrand-bound} to obtain
	\[
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(A(\gamma_1-\gamma_s)\big)
	\le
	\frac{1}{1-s}\cdot \frac{1-sc}{1-c}\cdot
	\frac{(1-s)^2}{(1-sc)^2}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1}Ah^{-1}A\big)
	=
	\frac{1-s}{1-c}\cdot\frac{1}{1-sc}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1}Ah^{-1}A\big).
	\]
	Plugging this into \eqref{eq:relent-path} and integrating gives
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_1,\Gamma_0)
	\le
	\frac{1}{1-c}\Big(\int_0^1 \frac{1-s}{1-sc}\,\,\mathrm d s\Big)\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1}Ah^{-1}A\big).
	\]
	which proves \eqref{eq:relent-bound}.

entries.statement:

	\[

		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(A_s\Big(\frac{1}{e^{\lambda(h_s-A_s)}-1}-\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h_s}-1}\Big)\Big).
	\end{equation}
	From \eqref{eq:A-upper-bound} we have
	\[

	\qquad
	A_s=(1-s)A \le (1-s)c\,h \le \frac{(1-s)c}{1-sc}\,h_s.

	\[

		\label{eq:lemma63-applied}

		\le
		\frac{1}{1-c_s}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big).
	\end{equation}
	Combining \eqref{eq:integrand-rewrite} and \eqref{eq:lemma63-applied} yields, for $s\in[0,1)$,
	\begin{equation}
		\label{eq:integrand-bound}
		\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(A(\gamma_1-\gamma_s)\big)
		\le
		\frac{1}{1-s}\cdot \frac{1}{1-c_s}\,\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big).
	\end{equation}
	First note that
	\[
	1-c_s = 1-\frac{(1-s)c}{1-sc}=\frac{1-c}{1-sc}

	&\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_s\big)
	=
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1/2}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_sh_s^{-1/2}\big)
	\\
	&\le \frac1{1-sc}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h_s^{-1/2}A_s\,h^{-1}A_sh_s^{-1/2}\big)
	=
	\frac1{1-sc}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1/2}A_s\,h_s^{-1}A_sh^{-1/2}\big)
	\\
	&\le \frac1{(1-sc)^2}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1/2}A_s\,h^{-1}A_sh^{-1/2}\big)

	Insert these two relations into \eqref{eq:integrand-bound} to obtain

entries.name: lem:shell-perturbed-free
entries.statement:
\label{lem:shell-perturbed-free}
	Let $f=f^*$ be a bounded one-body operator with
	\[
	\|f\|\le 1,
	\qquad
	a_f:=\bigl\|h^{-1/2}fh^{-1/2}\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}\le c_0,
	\]
	for some sufficiently small universal constant $c_0>0$.  Define
	\[
	h_t:=h-\frac t2 f,
	\qquad
	\Gamma_{0,t}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h_t)}}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h_t)})},
	\qquad |t|\le 1,
	\]
	and denote by $\gamma_{0,t}$ the one-body density matrix of $\Gamma_{0,t}$.
	Then uniformly for $|t|\le 1$,
	\begin{align}
		\lambda^2
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(
		(\mathcal N-N_0)^2\Gamma_{0,t}
		\Big)
		&\le C,
		\label{eq:shell-free-N2-local}
		\\
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\big)
		&\le C.
		\label{eq:shell-free-W-local}
	\end{align}
entries.proof:
We divide the proof into two steps.

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1. Centered number fluctuations for the perturbed free state.}
	Using \cite[Theorem 5.11]{LewNamRou-21}, we obtain
	\[
	\lambda^2
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\Big((\mathcal N-\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}))^2\Gamma_{0,t}\Big)
	\lesssim \mathrm{Tr}(h_t^{-2})\lesssim 1.
	\]
By \cite[Theorem 6.1]{LewNamRou-21}, together with
	Lemma~\ref{lem:relent-signedA} we obtain
\begin{align*}
\lambda\bigl|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t})-N_0\bigr|
&\le
\Big(\mathrm{Tr}\bigl|\sqrt{\lambda h}(\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)\sqrt{\lambda h}\bigr|^2\Big)^{1/2}
(\mathrm{Tr}h^{-2})^{1/2}
\le C a_f.
\end{align*}
Thus we find
	\[
	\lambda^2
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big((\mathcal N-N_0)^2\Gamma_{0,t}\Big)
	\le
	2\lambda^2
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big((\mathcal N-\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}))^2\Gamma_{0,t}\Big)
	+
	2\lambda^2\bigl(\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t})-N_0\bigr)^2
	\le C.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:shell-free-N2-local}.
	
	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 2. Expectation of the renormalized interaction.}
	We split
	\[
	W^{\rm re}
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}(\mathcal N-N_0)^2
	+
	W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}.
	\]
	The zero-mode part is already controlled by \eqref{eq:shell-free-N2-local}. For the
	nonzero-mode part, let
	\[
	M_k:=M_{e_k}
	\qquad (k\in\mathbb Z^2)
	\]
	denote multiplication by the normalized Fourier mode $e_k$. Then Wick's theorem yields
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:wick}
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2}
	\sum_{k\neq 0}#1{\widehat v_\beta}(k)
	\Big(
	|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k)|^2
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k})
	\Big).
	\end{equation}
	Indeed, recalling that
	\[
	W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
	\sum_{k\neq 0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\sum_{p,q\in\mathbb Z^2}
	a_{p+k}^*a_{q-k}^*a_q a_p,
	\]
	it is enough to compute the expectation of each quartic monomial in the quasi-free state
	$\Gamma_{0,t}$.
	
	For a gauge-invariant quasi-free state with one-body density matrix $\gamma_{0,t}$, one has
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a^*(f)a(g)\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
	=
	\langle g,\gamma_{0,t}f\rangle,
	\]
	and Wick's theorem gives
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_{q-k}^*a_q a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		&=
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr) \\
		&\quad+
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr).
	\end{align*}
	Thus \eqref{eq:wick} follows by a direct computation.
	
%	Now let $U_k$ be the multiplication operator
%	\[
%	(U_k\psi)(x)=e^{ik\cdot x}\psi(x).
%	\]
%	Since $U_k e_p=e_{p+k}$, we obtain
%	\[
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}e_{p+k}\rangle
%	=
%	\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle.
%	\]
%	Hence
%	\[
%	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\langle e_p,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k),
%	\]
%	and similarly
%	\[
%	\sum_{q\in\mathbb Z^2}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k}).
%	\]
%	Therefore the direct pairing contributes
%	\[
%	\sum_{p,q}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k})
%	=
%	\bigl|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)\bigr|^2,
%	\]
%	since $U_{-k}=U_k^*$ and $\gamma_{0,t}=\gamma_{0,t}^*$.
%	
%	For the exchange pairing, we get
%	\begin{align*}
%		&\sum_{p,q}
%		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr) \\
%		&\qquad=
%		\sum_{p,q}
%		\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle
%		\langle e_p,\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k} e_q\rangle
%		=
%		\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k}).
%	\end{align*}
%		Combining the two pairings and summing over $k\neq 0$, we arrive at
%	\[
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
%	\sum_{k\neq 0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)
%	\Big(
%	|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)|^2
%	+
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k})
%	\Big),
%	\]
%	as claimed.
%	
	We first treat the exchange term. Since $h_t\ge \frac12 h$ for $|t|\le1$, we have
	\[
	0\le \lambda\gamma_{0,t}=\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda h_t}-1}\le h_t^{-1}\le 2h^{-1}.
	\]
	Hence
	\begin{align*}
		&\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k})
		=
		\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}\bigr)
		\\
		&\le
		C\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}M_k h^{-1}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}\bigr)
		=
		C\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(h^{-1/2}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}M_kh^{-1/2}\bigr)
		\\
		&\le
		C\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(h^{-1}M_{-k}h^{-1}M_k\bigr)
		=
		C\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
		\le
		C\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2},
	\end{align*}
where the last step follows from Lemma~\ref{lem:sum}.
	
	For the direct term, we use $\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_0M_k)=0$ for $k\neq0$ and write
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k)=\mathrm{Tr}\bigl((\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)M_k\bigr).
	\]
	By \cite[Theorem 6.1]{LewNamRou-21}, together with
	Lemma~\ref{lem:relent-signedA},
	\[
	\lambda\,
	\bigl\|\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)\,\sqrt h\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\le C a_f.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\lambda |\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k)|
	\le
	\lambda\,
	\bigl\|\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)\,\sqrt h\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\bigl\|h^{-1/2}M_kh^{-1/2}\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\le
	C a_f\Bigl(\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}\Bigr)^{1/2},
	\]
	where we used
	\[
	\|h^{-1/2}M_k h^{-1/2}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
	=
	\sum_{q\in\mathbb Z^2}
	\frac{1}{(|q|^2+1)(|q+k|^2+1)}
	\le
	C\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	{\color{red}Since $\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)=\langle k\rangle^{-\beta}$ with $\beta>0$, both the direct and exchange
	contributions are summed against
	\[
	\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2},
	\]
	which is summable on $\mathbb Z^2$. Hence the two series converge uniformly in $|t|\le1$, and we conclude that}
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)\le C.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:shell-free-W-local}.

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1. Centered number fluctuations for the perturbed free state.}

	\]

	\[

	+
	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k})
	\Big).
	\end{equation}
	Indeed, recalling that
	\[
	W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
	\sum_{k\neq 0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\sum_{p,q\in\mathbb Z^2}
	a_{p+k}^*a_{q-k}^*a_q a_p,
	\]
	it is enough to compute the expectation of each quartic monomial in the quasi-free state
	$\Gamma_{0,t}$.
	
	For a gauge-invariant quasi-free state with one-body density matrix $\gamma_{0,t}$, one has
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a^*(f)a(g)\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
	=
	\langle g,\gamma_{0,t}f\rangle,
	\]
	and Wick's theorem gives
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_{q-k}^*a_q a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		&=
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr) \\
		&\quad+
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr).
	\end{align*}
	Thus \eqref{eq:wick} follows by a direct computation.
	
%	Now let $U_k$ be the multiplication operator
%	\[
%	(U_k\psi)(x)=e^{ik\cdot x}\psi(x).
%	\]
%	Since $U_k e_p=e_{p+k}$, we obtain
%	\[
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}e_{p+k}\rangle
%	=
%	\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle.
%	\]
%	Hence
%	\[
%	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\langle e_p,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k),
%	\]
%	and similarly
%	\[
%	\sum_{q\in\mathbb Z^2}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k}).
%	\]
%	Therefore the direct pairing contributes
%	\[
%	\sum_{p,q}
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k})
%	=
%	\bigl|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)\bigr|^2,
%	\]
%	since $U_{-k}=U_k^*$ and $\gamma_{0,t}=\gamma_{0,t}^*$.
%	
%	For the exchange pairing, we get
%	\begin{align*}
%		&\sum_{p,q}
%		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{p+k}^*a_q\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(a_{q-k}^*a_p\,\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr) \\
%		&\qquad=
%		\sum_{p,q}
%		\langle e_q,\gamma_{0,t}U_k e_p\rangle
%		\langle e_p,\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k} e_q\rangle
%		=
%		\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k}).
%	\end{align*}
%		Combining the two pairings and summing over $k\neq 0$, we arrive at
%	\[
%	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)
%	=
%	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
%	\sum_{k\neq 0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)
%	\Big(
%	|\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k)|^2
%	+
%	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}U_k\gamma_{0,t}U_{-k})
%	\Big),
%	\]
%	as claimed.
%	
	We first treat the exchange term. Since $h_t\ge \frac12 h$ for $|t|\le1$, we have
	\[
	0\le \lambda\gamma_{0,t}=\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda h_t}-1}\le h_t^{-1}\le 2h^{-1}.
	\]
	Hence
	\begin{align*}
		&\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k})
		=
		\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}M_k\gamma_{0,t}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}\bigr)
		\\
		&\le
		C\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}M_k h^{-1}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}^{1/2}\bigr)
		=
		C\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(h^{-1/2}M_{-k}\gamma_{0,t}M_kh^{-1/2}\bigr)
		\\
		&\le
		C\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(h^{-1}M_{-k}h^{-1}M_k\bigr)
		=
		C\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
		\le
		C\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2},
	\end{align*}
where the last step follows from Lemma~\ref{lem:sum}.
	
	For the direct term, we use $\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_0M_k)=0$ for $k\neq0$ and write
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k)=\mathrm{Tr}\bigl((\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)M_k\bigr).
	\]
	By \cite[Theorem 6.1]{LewNamRou-21}, together with
	Lemma~\ref{lem:relent-signedA},
	\[
	\lambda\,
	\bigl\|\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)\,\sqrt h\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\le C a_f.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\lambda |\mathrm{Tr}(\gamma_{0,t}M_k)|
	\le
	\lambda\,
	\bigl\|\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{0,t}-\gamma_0)\,\sqrt h\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\bigl\|h^{-1/2}M_kh^{-1/2}\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\le
	C a_f\Bigl(\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}\Bigr)^{1/2},
	\]
	where we used
	\[
	\|h^{-1/2}M_k h^{-1/2}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
	=
	\sum_{q\in\mathbb Z^2}
	\frac{1}{(|q|^2+1)(|q+k|^2+1)}
	\le
	C\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	{\color{red}Since $\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)=\langle k\rangle^{-\beta}$ with $\beta>0$, both the direct and exchange
	contributions are summed against
	\[
	\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2},
	\]
	which is summable on $\mathbb Z^2$. Hence the two series converge uniformly in $|t|\le1$, and we conclude that}
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\bigl(W_{\neq0}^{\rm re}\Gamma_{0,t}\bigr)\le C.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:shell-free-W-local}.

entries.name: lem:exch2d
entries.proof:
By the Gibbs variational principle relative to $\Gamma_{0,t}$,
	\[
	-\log\frac{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB})}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB})}
	=
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big).
	\]
	Taking the trial state $\Gamma_{0,t}$ yields
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t}\big).
	\]
	By Lemma~\ref{lem:shell-perturbed-free},
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:yukawa-free-energy-functional}, and
	\eqref{eq:yukawa-free-energy-upper} follows immediately.

entries.name: proof_9
entries.statement:
By the Gibbs variational principle relative to $\Gamma_{0,t}$,
	\[
	-\log\frac{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB})}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB})}
	=
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big).
	\]
	Taking the trial state $\Gamma_{0,t}$ yields
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t}\big).
	\]
	By Lemma~\ref{lem:shell-perturbed-free},
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:yukawa-free-energy-functional}, and
	\eqref{eq:yukawa-free-energy-upper} follows immediately.

entries.name: rem:missing-lower-bound-lemma11
entries.statement:
\label{rem:missing-lower-bound-lemma11}
	At this stage,  the lower bound
	\[
	0\le -\log\frac{Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}{Z_0}
	\]
	does \emph{not} follow directly from positivity of the interaction, because with the present
	renormalization one keeps the \(k\neq0\) part in genuine quartic form in order to avoid the divergent
	self-energy term \(\big(\sum_k \rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\big)\mathcal N\). Equivalently, in the sector-wise realization one has
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	=
	\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}+c_\lambda \mathcal N+C_\lambda,
	\]
	and for small \(\lambda\) the coefficient \(c_\lambda\) is negative. Thus \(W^{\mathrm{re}}\) is not manifestly
	nonnegative. Later, the required lower bound is recovered through the comparison with the finite-dimensional classical problem and the vanishing of the high-frequency remainders; see  Theorem~\ref{thm:lower-bound-classical-final}.

entries.name: prop:number-moments-log
entries.statement:
[Logarithmic moments of the number operator for the perturbed Gibbs states]
	\label{prop:number-moments-log}\label{prop:number-moments-log-safe}
	Let $B$,
	$\Gamma_{\lambda,t}$ and $\Gamma_{0,t}$ be as in Lemma \ref{lem:yukawa-lemma11-safe}. Then for every fixed $q>0$
	there exists $C_q>0$ such that, for all sufficiently small $\lambda\in(0,1/2)$,
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\lambda^q\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^q\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le C_q(1+|\log\lambda|)^q.
	\]
	In particular,
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le C(1+|\log\lambda|).
	\]
entries.proof:
Set
	\(
	L_\lambda:=1+|\log\lambda|.
	\)
	Choose once and for all
	\(
	s_0\in(0,\frac14].
	\)
	For every $|t|\le1$ and $|s|\le s_0$, define
	\[
	Z_{\lambda,t}(s):=\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB+s\lambda \mathcal N}\big),
	\qquad
	\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}:=\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB+s\lambda \mathcal N}}{Z_{\lambda,t}(s)},
	\]
	and
	\[
	\Gamma_{0,t,s}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB+s\lambda\mathcal N}}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB+s\lambda\mathcal N})}
	=
	\frac{e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h_{t,s})}}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h_{t,s})})},
	\]
	where
	\(
	h_{t,s}:=h-s-\frac t2 f.
	\)
	Since $\|f\|\le1$ and $h\ge1$, we have
	\[
	h_{t,s}\ge h-\Bigl(s_0+\frac12\Bigr)\ge \frac14 h>0,
	\]
	so the free state $\Gamma_{0,t,s}$ is well defined.

	
	Set
	\(
	A_{t,s}:=s+\frac t2 f.
	\)
	Then
	\[
	h_{t,s}=h-A_{t,s},
	\qquad
	A_{t,s}\le \Bigl(s_0+\frac12\Bigr)h\le \frac34 h.
	\]
	Moreover,
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(h^{-1}A_{t,s}h^{-1}A_{t,s}\big)\le C,
	\]
	uniformly for $|t|\le1$ and $|s|\le s_0$, because $h^{-1}\in\mathfrak S^2$ and
	$\|h^{-1/2}fh^{-1/2}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}\le c_0$. The proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:shell-perturbed-free}
	uses only the lower bound $h_{t,s}\gtrsim h$ and the control of
	$\mathrm{Tr}(h^{-1}A_{t,s}h^{-1}A_{t,s})$, so it applies verbatim with $h_t$ replaced by $h_{t,s}$.
	Therefore
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:free-W-ts}
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t,s}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1,\ |s|\le s_0.
	\end{equation}

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1. Uniform first and second particle-number bounds.}
	By the Gibbs variational principle relative to $\Gamma_{0,t,s}$,
	\[
	\mathcal H(\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s},\Gamma_{0,t,s})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}\big)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t,s}\big).
	\]
	Hence \eqref{eq:free-W-ts} implies
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1,\ |s|\le s_0.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	W^{\mathrm{re}}=\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}+c_\lambda\mathcal N+C_\lambda,
	\]
	together with
	\[
	\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}
	\ge
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{2}\,\mathcal N(\mathcal N-1)
	\ge
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\,\mathcal N^2-C\lambda^2,
	\]
from Lemma \ref{lem:periodic-yukawa-positive},	we infer
	\[
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	c_\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	C_\lambda
	\le C.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	c_\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	=
	\alpha_0\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	-
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr),
	\]
	with $\alpha_0=\frac12(2\pi)^{-2}$, we discard the positive term
	$\alpha_0\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})$ and infer
	\[
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	-
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)
	+
	\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)^2
	\le C.
	\]
	By Cauchy--Schwarz,
	\[
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	\bigl(\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)^{1/2},
	\]
	and another application of Young's inequality yields
	\[
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)
	\le
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{8}\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	C(\lambda N_0)^2.
	\]
Thus
	\[
	\sup_{\substack{|t|\le1\\ |s|\le s_0}}
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	C L_\lambda^2,
	\]
	and therefore
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perturbed-first-moment-ts}
	\sup_{\substack{|t|\le1\\ |s|\le s_0}}
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	C L_\lambda.
	\end{equation}

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 2. Exponential moments and all fixed moments.}
	Since both $\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}$ and $B$ commute with $\mathcal N$, the map
	$s\mapsto Z_{\lambda,t}(s)$ is differentiable and
	\[
	\frac{\,\mathrm d}{\,\mathrm d s}\log Z_{\lambda,t}(s)
	=
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}).
	\]
	By \eqref{eq:perturbed-first-moment-ts}, for every $|t|\le1$ and $|s|\le s_0$,
	\[
	\left|
	\log\frac{Z_{\lambda,t}(s)}{Z_{\lambda,t}(0)}
	\right|=
	\left|\int_0^s\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,r})\,\,\mathrm d r\right|
	\le
	C L_\lambda |s|.
	\]
	Define
	\(
	X_\lambda:=\frac{\lambda\mathcal N}{L_\lambda}.
	\)
	Then for every $\tau\in[0,s_0]$,
	\[
	\log\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	=
	\log\frac{Z_{\lambda,t}(\tau/L_\lambda)}{Z_{\lambda,t}(0)}
	\le C\tau,
	\]
	hence
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)\le e^{C\tau}
	\qquad\text{for all }\tau\in[0,s_0].
	\]
	Fix $q>0$ and $\tau_*:=s_0/2$. Using the elementary inequality
	\(
	x^q\le C_{q,\tau_*}e^{\tau_*x}
	\) for $x\geq0$,
	we conclude that
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}(X_\lambda^q\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le
	C_{q,\tau_*}\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau_*X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	\le
	C_q.
	\]
	Equivalently,
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\lambda^q\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^q\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le C_qL_\lambda^q.
	\]
	This proves the proposition.

entries.name: eq:free-W-ts
entries.statement:
Set
	\(
	L_\lambda:=1+|\log\lambda|.
	\)
	Choose once and for all
	\(
	s_0\in(0,\frac14].
	\)
	For every $|t|\le1$ and $|s|\le s_0$, define
	\[
	Z_{\lambda,t}(s):=\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB+s\lambda \mathcal N}\big),
	\qquad
	\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}:=\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB+s\lambda \mathcal N}}{Z_{\lambda,t}(s)},
	\]
	and
	\[
	\Gamma_{0,t,s}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB+s\lambda\mathcal N}}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB+s\lambda\mathcal N})}

	$\mathrm{Tr}(h^{-1}A_{t,s}h^{-1}A_{t,s})$, so it applies verbatim with $h_t$ replaced by $h_{t,s}$.

	\begin{equation}\label{eq:free-W-ts}
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t,s}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1,\ |s|\le s_0.
	\end{equation}

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1. Uniform first and second particle-number bounds.}
	By the Gibbs variational principle relative to $\Gamma_{0,t,s}$,
	\[
	\mathcal H(\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s},\Gamma_{0,t,s})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}\big)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{0,t,s}\big).
	\]
	Hence \eqref{eq:free-W-ts} implies
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}\big)\le C
	\qquad\text{uniformly for }|t|\le1,\ |s|\le s_0.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	W^{\mathrm{re}}=\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}+c_\lambda\mathcal N+C_\lambda,
	\]
	together with
	\[
	\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}
	\ge
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{2}\,\mathcal N(\mathcal N-1)
	\ge
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\,\mathcal N^2-C\lambda^2,
	\]
from Lemma \ref{lem:periodic-yukawa-positive},	we infer
	\[
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	c_\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	C_\lambda
	\le C.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	c_\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	=
	\alpha_0\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	-
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr),
	\]
	with $\alpha_0=\frac12(2\pi)^{-2}$, we discard the positive term
	$\alpha_0\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})$ and infer
	\[
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}\lambda^2}{4}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	-
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)
	+
	\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)^2
	\le C.
	\]
	By Cauchy--Schwarz,
	\[
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	\bigl(\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)^{1/2},
	\]
	and another application of Young's inequality yields
	\[
	2\alpha_0(\lambda N_0)\bigl(\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})\bigr)
	\le
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{8}\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	+
	C(\lambda N_0)^2.
	\]
Thus
	\[
	\sup_{\substack{|t|\le1\\ |s|\le s_0}}
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	C L_\lambda^2,
	\]
	and therefore
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:perturbed-first-moment-ts}
	\sup_{\substack{|t|\le1\\ |s|\le s_0}}
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s})
	\le
	C L_\lambda.
	\end{equation}

	\smallskip
	\noindent\textbf{Step 2. Exponential moments and all fixed moments.}
	Since both $\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}$ and $B$ commute with $\mathcal N$, the map
	$s\mapsto Z_{\lambda,t}(s)$ is differentiable and
	\[
	\frac{\,\mathrm d}{\,\mathrm d s}\log Z_{\lambda,t}(s)
	=
	\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,s}).
	\]
	By \eqref{eq:perturbed-first-moment-ts}, for every $|t|\le1$ and $|s|\le s_0$,
	\[
	\left|
	\log\frac{Z_{\lambda,t}(s)}{Z_{\lambda,t}(0)}
	\right|=
	\left|\int_0^s\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t,r})\,\,\mathrm d r\right|
	\le
	C L_\lambda |s|.
	\]
	Define
	\(
	X_\lambda:=\frac{\lambda\mathcal N}{L_\lambda}.
	\)
	Then for every $\tau\in[0,s_0]$,
	\[
	\log\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	=
	\log\frac{Z_{\lambda,t}(\tau/L_\lambda)}{Z_{\lambda,t}(0)}
	\le C\tau,
	\]
	hence
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)\le e^{C\tau}
	\qquad\text{for all }\tau\in[0,s_0].
	\]
	Fix $q>0$ and $\tau_*:=s_0/2$. Using the elementary inequality
	\(
	x^q\le C_{q,\tau_*}e^{\tau_*x}
	\) for $x\geq0$,
	we conclude that
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}(X_\lambda^q\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le
	C_{q,\tau_*}\sup_{|t|\le1}\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(e^{\tau_*X_\lambda}\Gamma_{\lambda,t}\big)
	\le
	C_q.
	\]
	Equivalently,
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\lambda^q\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^q\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le C_qL_\lambda^q.
	\]
	This proves the proposition.

entries.name: prop:one-body-HS-safe
entries.proof:
Set again
	\(
	L_\lambda:=1+|\log\lambda|.
	\)
	By Lemma~\ref{lem:yukawa-lemma11-safe},
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	+
	\mathrm{Tr}(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\le C
	\qquad\text{for all }|t|\le1.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	W^{\mathrm{re}}=\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}+c_\lambda\mathcal N+C_\lambda,
	\]
	with
	\[
	c_\lambda
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}(1-2N_0),
	\qquad
	C_\lambda
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}N_0^2,
	\]
	{#1{and the pointwise nonnegativity of the fractional-Bessel kernel, we have}}
	\[
	\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}\ge0,
	\qquad
	C_\lambda\ge0.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(W^{\mathrm{re}}\Gamma_{\lambda,t})
	\ge
	c_\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t}),
	\]
	and therefore
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	\le
	C+|c_\lambda|\,\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t}).
	\]
	Now
	\[
	|c_\lambda|
	\le C\lambda^2(1+N_0)
	\le C\lambda L_\lambda,
	\]
	while Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} gives
	\[
	\sup_{|t|\le1}\lambda\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma_{\lambda,t})\le CL_\lambda.
	\]
	Combining the last two bounds yields \eqref{eq:relative-entropy-log2}.

		Here
		\(
		h_t=h-\frac t2 f.
		\)
	Applying \cite[Theorem~6.1]{LewNamRou-21} with $h_t$ in place of $h$ gives
	\[
	\lambda^2
	\bigl\|
	\sqrt{h_t}\,(\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(1)}-\Gamma_{0,t}^{(1)})\,\sqrt{h_t}
	\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
	\le
	4\,\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})
	\bigl(\sqrt2+\sqrt{\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_{\lambda,t},\Gamma_{0,t})}\bigr)^2.
	\]
	Since $h\le 2h_t$, we obtain \eqref{eq:one-body-HS-safe}.
By the Hilbert--Schmidt Cauchy--Schwarz inequality,
	\[
	\lambda\left|
	\mathrm{Tr}\big[(\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(1)}-\Gamma_{0,t}^{(1)})\big]
	\right|
	\le
	\lambda\|h^{-1}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}\,
	\bigl\|
	\sqrt h\,(\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(1)}-\Gamma_{0,t}^{(1)})\,\sqrt h
	\bigr\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
	\le
	CL_\lambda^2,
	\]
	uniformly in $|t|\le1$. This proves \eqref{eq:number-difference-global-safe}.

entries.name: proof_14
entries.statement:

	\(
	L_\lambda:=1+|\log\lambda|.

	\[

entries.name: lem:first-kinetic-log2
entries.statement:
[First kinetic moment under logarithmic relative entropy]

	\label{lem:first-kinetic-log2}
	For every fixed $t\in(0,1)$ there exists $C_t>0$ such that
	\[
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	\frac{C_t}{t}
	+
	\frac{C}{t}\,\lambda(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	In particular,
	\[
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\big)\le C.
	\]
entries.proof:
For every self-adjoint operator $X$ such that $\mathrm{Tr}(e^X\Gamma_0)<\infty$, the entropy inequality gives
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(X\Gamma_\lambda)
	\le
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_\lambda,\Gamma_0)+\log\mathrm{Tr}(e^X\Gamma_0).
	\]
	Choose
	\[
	X_t:=t\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h),
	\qquad 0<t<1.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	t\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_\lambda,\Gamma_0)
	+
	\log\frac{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-(1-t)\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}.
	\]
	By Proposition~\ref{prop:one-body-HS-safe}, the entropy term is bounded by
	\[
	\mathcal{H}(\Gamma_\lambda,\Gamma_0)\le C(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	It remains to estimate the free partition-function ratio.
	
	Set
	\[
	\Phi(s):=\log\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-s\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})
	=
	-\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\log(1-e^{-sh(p)}),
	\qquad s>0.
	\]
Here we used \cite[Appendix A]{LewNamRou-15}.
	Then
	\[
	\Phi'(s)
	=
	-\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac{h(p)}{e^{sh(p)}-1},
	\]
	so that
	\[
	\log\frac{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-(1-t)\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}
	=
	-\int_{(1-t)\lambda}^{\lambda}\Phi'(s)\,\,\mathrm d s
	=
	\int_{1-t}^{1}
	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac{\lambda h(p)}{e^{r\lambda h(p)}-1}\,\,\mathrm d r,
	\]
	after the change of variables $s=r\lambda$.
	
	Fix $t\in(0,1)$ and $r\in[1-t,1]$. Split the sum into the regions $h(p)\le\lambda^{-1}$ and $h(p)>\lambda^{-1}$.
	If $h(p)\le\lambda^{-1}$, then
	\[
	\frac{\lambda h(p)}{e^{r\lambda h(p)}-1}\le C_t,
	\]
	hence
	\[
	\sum_{h(p)\le\lambda^{-1}}\frac{\lambda h(p)}{e^{r\lambda h(p)}-1}
	\le
	C_t\,\#\{p\in\mathbb Z^2:\ h(p)\le\lambda^{-1}\}
	\le C_t\lambda^{-1}.
	\]
	If $h(p)>\lambda^{-1}$, then
	\[
	\frac{\lambda h(p)}{e^{r\lambda h(p)}-1}
	\le
	C_t\lambda h(p)e^{-c_t\lambda h(p)},
	\]
	and comparison with the corresponding Gaussian integral on $\mathbb R^2$ gives
	\[
	\sum_{h(p)>\lambda^{-1}}\frac{\lambda h(p)}{e^{r\lambda h(p)}-1}
	\le
	C_t\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\lambda h(p)e^{-c_t\lambda h(p)}
	\le C_t\lambda^{-1}.
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\log\frac{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-(1-t)\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda\mathrm d\Gamma(h)})}
	\le C_t\lambda^{-1}.
	\]
	Combining the previous estimates, we arrive at
	\[
	t\lambda\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)+C_t\lambda^{-1}.
	\]
	Multiplying by $\lambda$ yields
	\[
	t\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	C\lambda(1+|\log\lambda|^2)+C_t,
	\]
	which proves the first estimate. Choosing, for instance, $t=1/2$ gives the uniform bound in the second display.

entries.name: lem:holder
entries.statement:
\label{lem:holder}
	Let
	\(
	\theta\in(0,1).
	\)
	Then
	\begin{align}
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\bigl[\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma_\lambda\bigr]
		&\le
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\bigl[\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\bigr]^\theta\,
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Bigl[\mathcal N^{\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}}\Gamma_\lambda\Bigr]^{1-\theta}.
		\label{eq:main-estimate}
	\end{align}
Moreover, write $L_\lambda:=1+|\log\lambda|$ and $\mathbb{H}_s:=\mathrm d\Gamma(h^s)$ for $s\in(0,1)$. Then for $s_1,s_2\in(0,1)$ with $\theta=s_1+s_2\in(0,1)$
\begin{equation}
			\label{eq:shell-energy-number-moment-local}
			\lambda^4
			\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(
			\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\Gamma_\lambda
			\Big)
			\le
			C\lambda^{2-\theta}L_\lambda^2.
		\end{equation}
	%	Consequently,
	%	\begin{align}
		%		\lambda^4\Lambda^2
		%		\Bigl(
		%		\mathrm{Tr}\!\bigl[\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma_\lambda\bigr]+1
		%		\Bigr)
		%		\le
		%		\lambda^4\Lambda^2
		%		\Biggl(
		%		\mathrm{Tr}\!\bigl[\mathrm d\Gamma(h)\Gamma_\lambda\bigr]^\theta\,
		%		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Bigl[\mathcal N^{\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}}\Gamma_\lambda\Bigr]^{1-\theta}
		%		+1
		%		\Biggr).
		%		\label{eq:lambda-Lambda-bound}
		%	\end{align}
entries.proof:
Set
	\[
	A:=\mathrm d\Gamma(h),\qquad \Gamma:=\Gamma_\lambda,\qquad r:=\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}.
	\]
	If either $\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)=\infty$ or $\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma)=\infty$, then the desired inequality is
	trivial. Hence we may assume that both quantities are finite.
	
		We use the particle-number decomposition
		\[
		\mathfrak F=\bigoplus_{n=0}^\infty \mathfrak{H}_n,\qquad \mathfrak{H}_n:=\mathfrak{H}^{\otimes_s n},
		\]
	and let $\Pi_n$ be the orthogonal projection onto $\mathfrak{H}_n$. By the definition of second
	quantization,
	\[
	A=0\oplus \bigoplus_{n\ge 1} A_n,
	\qquad
	A_n:=\sum_{j=1}^n h_j
	\quad\text{on }\mathfrak{H}_n,
	\]
	whereas
	\[
	\mathcal N\big|_{\mathfrak{H}_n}=n\,I_{\mathfrak{H}_n}.
	\]
	Moreover, since $[\mathcal N,\Gamma]=0$, the state $\Gamma$ is block-diagonal with respect to the
	particle-number decomposition:
	\[
	\Gamma=\bigoplus_{n=0}^\infty \Gamma_n,
	\qquad
	\Gamma_n:=\Pi_n\Gamma\Pi_n\ge 0.
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	=
	\sum_{n\ge 0} n^{2-\theta}\mathrm{Tr}(A_n^\theta\Gamma_n).
	\]
	
	Fix $t>0$. For every $x\ge 0$, Young's inequality gives
	\[
	n^{2-\theta}x^\theta
	=
	(tx)^\theta
	\Big(t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r\Big)^{1-\theta}
	\le
	\theta\,tx+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r.
	\]
	Applying the functional calculus to the positive self-adjoint operator $A_n$, we obtain
	the quadratic-form inequality on $\mathfrak{H}_n$,
	\[
	n^{2-\theta}A_n^\theta
	\le
	\theta\,tA_n+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r I_{\mathfrak{H}_n}.
	\]
	Taking the trace against $\Gamma_n\ge 0$ and summing over $n$, we infer that
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	\le
	\theta\,t\,\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)
	+
	(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma).
	\]
	
	Now write
	\[
	a:=\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma),
	\qquad
	b:=\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma).
	\]
	If $a=0$ or $b=0$, then the desired inequality is immediate. Hence we may assume that
	$a,b>0$ and choose
	\[
	t=\Big(\frac{b}{a}\Big)^{1-\theta}.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	\theta\,ta+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}b
	=
	\theta\,a^\theta b^{1-\theta}+(1-\theta)a^\theta b^{1-\theta}
	=
	a^\theta b^{1-\theta}.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)^\theta\,\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma)^{1-\theta},
	\]
	which is exactly the first result.
	
	On the $n$-particle sector,
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_s=\sum_{j=1}^n h_j^s
	\le
	\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^n h_j\Bigr)^s n^{1-s}
	=
	\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^s\mathcal N^{1-s},
	\qquad 0<s<1,
	\]
	so, using $[\mathrm d\Gamma(h),\mathcal N]=0$,
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}
	\le
	\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^\theta\mathcal N^{2-\theta}.
	\]
	Applying the first part of the lemma and then Lemma~\ref{lem:first-kinetic-log2} together with Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} (with $t=0$ and $q=\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}$) yields
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	C\,\lambda^{-2\theta}\,\lambda^{-(2-\theta)}L_\lambda^{2-\theta}.
	\]
	Since $L_\lambda\ge 1$ and $2-\theta\le 2$, multiplying by $\lambda^4$ gives \eqref{eq:shell-energy-number-moment-local}.
	%	If $\theta=0$, the conclusion is immediate. If $a=0$ or $b=0$, the same estimate follows
	%	from
	%	\[
	%	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	%	\le
	%	\theta\,t\,a+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}b
	%	\]
	%	by letting $t\to\infty$ or $t\to0$, respectively.
	
	%	Finally, \emph{(5.10)} follows directly from \emph{(5.9)} by multiplying both sides by
	%	$\lambda^4\Lambda^2$ and adding $1$.

entries.name: proof_18
entries.statement:
Set
	\[
	A:=\mathrm d\Gamma(h),\qquad \Gamma:=\Gamma_\lambda,\qquad r:=\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}.
	\]
	If either $\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)=\infty$ or $\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma)=\infty$, then the desired inequality is

	\[

	\Big(t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r\Big)^{1-\theta}
	\le
	\theta\,tx+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r.
	\]
	Applying the functional calculus to the positive self-adjoint operator $A_n$, we obtain
	the quadratic-form inequality on $\mathfrak{H}_n$,
	\[
	n^{2-\theta}A_n^\theta
	\le
	\theta\,tA_n+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}n^r I_{\mathfrak{H}_n}.
	\]
	Taking the trace against $\Gamma_n\ge 0$ and summing over $n$, we infer that
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	\le
	\theta\,t\,\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)
	+
	(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma).
	\]
	
	Now write
	\[
	a:=\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma),
	\qquad
	b:=\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma).
	\]
	If $a=0$ or $b=0$, then the desired inequality is immediate. Hence we may assume that
	$a,b>0$ and choose
	\[
	t=\Big(\frac{b}{a}\Big)^{1-\theta}.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	\theta\,ta+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}b
	=
	\theta\,a^\theta b^{1-\theta}+(1-\theta)a^\theta b^{1-\theta}
	=
	a^\theta b^{1-\theta}.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta \mathcal N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	\le
	\mathrm{Tr}(A\Gamma)^\theta\,\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^r\Gamma)^{1-\theta},
	\]
	which is exactly the first result.
	
	On the $n$-particle sector,
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_s=\sum_{j=1}^n h_j^s
	\le
	\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^n h_j\Bigr)^s n^{1-s}
	=
	\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^s\mathcal N^{1-s},
	\qquad 0<s<1,
	\]
	so, using $[\mathrm d\Gamma(h),\mathcal N]=0$,
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}
	\le
	\mathrm d\Gamma(h)^\theta\mathcal N^{2-\theta}.
	\]
	Applying the first part of the lemma and then Lemma~\ref{lem:first-kinetic-log2} together with Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} (with $t=0$ and $q=\frac{2-\theta}{1-\theta}$) yields
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\Gamma_\lambda\big)
	\le
	C\,\lambda^{-2\theta}\,\lambda^{-(2-\theta)}L_\lambda^{2-\theta}.
	\]
	Since $L_\lambda\ge 1$ and $2-\theta\le 2$, multiplying by $\lambda^4$ gives \eqref{eq:shell-energy-number-moment-local}.
	%	If $\theta=0$, the conclusion is immediate. If $a=0$ or $b=0$, the same estimate follows
	%	from
	%	\[
	%	\mathrm{Tr}(A^\theta N^{2-\theta}\Gamma)
	%	\le
	%	\theta\,t\,a+(1-\theta)t^{-\frac{\theta}{1-\theta}}b
	%	\]
	%	by letting $t\to\infty$ or $t\to0$, respectively.
	
	%	Finally, \emph{(5.10)} follows directly from \emph{(5.9)} by multiplying both sides by
	%	$\lambda^4\Lambda^2$ and adding $1$.

entries.name: thm:correlation-intro
prop:second-cutoff-decomp
entries.statement:
[Second cutoff decomposition of $HF_{\neq 0}$]
	\label{prop:second-cutoff-decomp}
	For every state $\Gamma$ on $\mathfrak F(H)$, one has
	\[
	\mathcal E_{\rm tail}(\Gamma)
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2}
	\mathrm{Tr}_{H^{\otimes_s 2}}
	\!\Big[
	\bigl(
	R_1 V R_1 + R_1 V \Pi_1 + \Pi_1 V R_1
	\bigr)\Gamma^{(2)}
	\Big]
	-
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
	\mathrm{Tr}_{H^{\otimes_s 2}}(R_1\Gamma^{(2)}).
	\]
	In particular, since $V\ge 0$,
	\[
	\mathcal E_{\rm tail}(\Gamma)
	\ge
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2}
	\mathrm{Tr}_{H^{\otimes_s 2}}
	\!\Big[
	\bigl(
	R_1 V \Pi_1 + \Pi_1 V R_1
	\bigr)\Gamma^{(2)}
	\Big]
	-
	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
	\mathrm{Tr}_{H^{\otimes_s 2}}(R_1\Gamma^{(2)}).
	\]
entries.proof:
Using
	\[
	V_{\neq 0}
	=
	V-\frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\,1_{H^{\otimes_s 2}},
	\]
	we get
	\[
	V_{\neq 0}-\Pi_1V_{\neq 0}\Pi_1
	=
	V-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	-
	\frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}R_1.
	\]
	Next,
	\[
	V-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	=
	(\Pi_1+R_1)V(\Pi_1+R_1)-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	=
	R_1VR_1+R_1V\Pi_1+\Pi_1VR_1.
	\]
	Substituting this into the definition of $\mathcal E_{\rm tail}$ yields the claimed identity.
	Since $V\ge 0$, we have $R_1VR_1\ge 0$, and the lower bound follows immediately.

entries.name: proof_21
entries.statement:
Using
	\[
	V_{\neq 0}
	=
	V-\frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}\,1_{H^{\otimes_s 2}},
	\]
	we get
	\[
	V_{\neq 0}-\Pi_1V_{\neq 0}\Pi_1
	=
	V-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	-
	\frac{1}{(2\pi)^2}R_1.
	\]
	Next,
	\[
	V-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	=
	(\Pi_1+R_1)V(\Pi_1+R_1)-\Pi_1V\Pi_1
	=
	R_1VR_1+R_1V\Pi_1+\Pi_1VR_1.
	\]
	Substituting this into the definition of $\mathcal E_{\rm tail}$ yields the claimed identity.
	Since $V\ge 0$, we have $R_1VR_1\ge 0$, and the lower bound follows immediately.

entries.name: rem:surviving-channels
entries.statement:
[Surviving channels after the second cutoff]
	\label{rem:surviving-channels}
	After discarding the positive contribution $R_1VR_1$, the only channels which remain in the lower
	bound are
	\[
	(P,P_1),\quad (P_1,P),\quad (P_1,P_1),
	\]
	coming from the shell term, and
	\[
	(P,Q_1),\quad (P_1,Q_1),\quad (Q_1,P),\quad (Q_1,P_1),
	\]
	coming from the mixed tail term. In other words, the second cutoff isolates two qualitatively different
	mechanisms: shell interactions, where both legs remain below $\Lambda_1$, and mixed tail channels,
	where exactly one leg lies in $Q_1H$ after the positive $Q_1Q_1$ contribution has been discarded.
	The pure $Q_1Q_1$ channel is entirely contained in $R_1VR_1$ and therefore needs no further
	estimate.

entries.name: cor:HF0-vanishes
entries.statement:
[Quantitative vanishing of the zero-mode remainder]
	\label{cor:HF0-vanishes}
	Let
	\(
	\Lambda=\lambda^{-\alpha},
	0<\alpha<\frac34.
	\)
	Then
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:HF0-vanishes-rate}
		|HF_0(\Gamma_\lambda)|
		\le
		C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)(\lambda^{\alpha/2}+\lambda^{3/4-\alpha}).
	\end{equation}
	In particular,
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:HF0-vanishes}
		HF_0(\Gamma_\lambda)\longrightarrow 0
		\qquad (\lambda\downarrow0).
	\end{equation}

entries.name: prop:high-frequency-fluctuations
entries.statement:
[High-frequency number fluctuations]
	\label{prop:high-frequency-fluctuations}
	Let $\Lambda=\lambda^{-\alpha}$ for $\alpha\in(0,3/4)$.
	Then
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:high-frequency-fluctuation-bound}
		\lambda^2
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F(H)}
		\Big[
		(\mathcal N_Q-N_{0,Q})^2\,\Gamma_\lambda
		\Big]
		\le
		C
		(1+|\log\lambda|^2)(\Lambda^{-1}
		+
		\lambda^{\frac32}\Lambda^2)
		.
	\end{equation}

entries.name: lem:NQ-double-commutator-direct
entries.statement:
[Double commutator structure for $\mathcal N_Q$]
	\label{lem:NQ-double-commutator-direct}
	%	To avoid confusion with the cutoff parameter, write
	%	\[
	%	\mathcal N:=\mathrm d\Gamma(1),
	%	\qquad
	%	\mathcal N_Q:=\mathrm d\Gamma(Q),
	%	\qquad
	%	T:=\mathrm d\Gamma(-\Delta)=\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}|p|^2 a_p^\ast a_p .
	%	\]
	%	Let
	%	\[
	%	W_{\neq0}^{\mathrm{re}}
	%	=
	%	\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
	%	\sum_{k\neq0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\sum_{p,q\in\mathbb Z^2}
	%	a_{p+k}^\ast a_{q-k}^\ast a_q a_p ,
	%	\]
	%	with Yukawa symbol
	%	\[
	%	\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)=\frac{1}{1+|k|^2},\qquad k\in\mathbb Z^2.
	%	\]
	The following hold.
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textnormal{(i) Exact formula.}
	For
	\[
	\mathcal N_Q=\mathrm d\Gamma(Q),
	\qquad
	M_{p,q,k}:=a_{p+k}^\ast a_{q-k}^\ast a_q a_p,
	\]
	define
	\[
	\Delta_{p,q,k}:=Q_{p+k}+Q_{q-k}-Q_q-Q_p,
	\qquad
	Q_r:=\mathbf 1_{\{h(r)>\Lambda^2\}}\in\{0,1\}.
	\]
	Then, as a quadratic form on finite-particle vectors,
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:exact-double-commutator-DQ}
		[\mathcal N_Q,[W^{\mathrm{re}},\mathcal N_Q]]
		=
		-\frac{\lambda^2}{2(2\pi)^2}
		\sum_{k\neq0}#1{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\sum_{p,q\in\mathbb Z^2}
		\Delta_{p,q,k}^2\, M_{p,q,k}.
	\end{equation}
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textnormal{(ii) Blockwise values of $\Delta_{p,q,k}$.}
	With the $A/B$-block decomposition introduced earlier, one has
	\[
	\Delta_{p,q,k}=0
	\quad\text{for}\quad
	A1,\ A4,\ A7,\ B2,\ B4,\ B9,
	\]
	and
	\[
	|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=1
	\quad\text{for}\quad
	A2,\ A3,\ A5,\ A6,\ B3,\ B6,\ B7,\ B8,
	\]
	while
	\[
	|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=2
	\quad\text{for}\quad
	B1,\ B5.
	\]
	Hence only the ten blocks
	\[
	A2,\ A3,\ A5,\ A6,\ B1,\ B3,\ B5,\ B6,\ B7,\ B8
	\]
	survive in \eqref{eq:exact-double-commutator-DQ}.
	
	%	\medskip
	%	
	%	\noindent\textnormal{(iii)}
	%	set
	%	\[
	%	h(p)=|p|^2+1,\qquad \mathbb{H}_s:=\mathrm d\Gamma(h^s)=\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}h(p)^s a_p^\ast a_p .
	%	\]
	%	Assume $s>\frac12$. Then there exists $C_s>0$ such that, as a quadratic form on $\mathfrak F_{\rm fin}$,
	%	\begin{equation}\label{eq:NQ-double-commutator-Hs}
		%		\pm\,[\mathcal N_Q,[W_{\neq0}^{\rm re},\mathcal N_Q]]
		%		\ \le\
		%		C_s\,\lambda^2\,(\mathbb{H}_s+\mathcal N+1)^2.
		%	\end{equation}
	%	In particular, taking $s=1$ gives
	%	\[
	%	(\mathbb{H}_1+\mathcal N+1)^2 \lesssim (T+\mathcal N+1)^2,
	%	\qquad \mathbb{H}_1=\mathrm d\Gamma(h)=T+m\mathcal N,
	%	\]
entries.proof:
We first prove part~\textnormal{(i)}. Since
	\[
	\mathcal N_Q=\sum_{r\in\mathbb Z^2}Q_r\,a_r^\ast a_r,
	\]
	the CCR imply
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:DQ-on-creation-annihilation}
		[\mathcal N_Q,a_j^\ast]=Q_j a_j^\ast,
		\qquad
		[\mathcal N_Q,a_j]=-Q_j a_j.
	\end{equation}
	Hence,
	the Leibniz rule gives
	\[
	[\mathcal N_Q,M_{p,q,k}]
	=
	(Q_{p+k}+Q_{q-k}-Q_q-Q_p)M_{p,q,k}
	=
	\Delta_{p,q,k}M_{p,q,k},
	\]
	which implies that
	\[
	[\mathcal N_Q,[M_{p,q,k},\mathcal N_Q]]
	=
	[\mathcal N_Q,-\Delta_{p,q,k}M_{p,q,k}]
	=
	-\Delta_{p,q,k}^2M_{p,q,k}.
	\]
	Summing over $p,q\in\mathbb Z^2$ and $k\neq0$ yields
	\eqref{eq:exact-double-commutator-DQ}.
	
	\medskip
	
	For part~\textnormal{(ii)}, it is convenient to record the contribution of a single leg to
	$\Delta_{p,q,k}$.
	Therefore $\Delta_{p,q,k}=0$ exactly when the two increments are either $0+0$, $1-1$, or
	$-1+1$, which gives the blocks
	\[
	A1,\ A4,\ A7,\ B2,\ B4,\ B9.
	\]
	Likewise, $|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=1$ when exactly one leg crosses the cutoff, namely on
	\[
	A2,\ A3,\ A5,\ A6,\ B3,\ B6,\ B7,\ B8,
	\]
	and $|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=2$ when both legs cross in the same direction, namely on
	\[
	B1,\ B5.
	\]
	This proves the claimed list of surviving blocks.

entries.name: eq:DQ-on-creation-annihilation
entries.statement:
We first prove part~\textnormal{(i)}. Since
	\[
	\mathcal N_Q=\sum_{r\in\mathbb Z^2}Q_r\,a_r^\ast a_r,
	\]
	the CCR imply
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:DQ-on-creation-annihilation}
		[\mathcal N_Q,a_j^\ast]=Q_j a_j^\ast,
		\qquad
		[\mathcal N_Q,a_j]=-Q_j a_j.
	\end{equation}
	Hence,
	the Leibniz rule gives
	\[
	[\mathcal N_Q,M_{p,q,k}]
	=
	(Q_{p+k}+Q_{q-k}-Q_q-Q_p)M_{p,q,k}
	=
	\Delta_{p,q,k}M_{p,q,k},
	\]
	which implies that
	\[
	[\mathcal N_Q,[M_{p,q,k},\mathcal N_Q]]
	=
	[\mathcal N_Q,-\Delta_{p,q,k}M_{p,q,k}]
	=
	-\Delta_{p,q,k}^2M_{p,q,k}.
	\]
	Summing over $p,q\in\mathbb Z^2$ and $k\neq0$ yields
	\eqref{eq:exact-double-commutator-DQ}.
	
	\medskip
	
	For part~\textnormal{(ii)}, it is convenient to record the contribution of a single leg to
	$\Delta_{p,q,k}$.
	Therefore $\Delta_{p,q,k}=0$ exactly when the two increments are either $0+0$, $1-1$, or
	$-1+1$, which gives the blocks
	\[
	A1,\ A4,\ A7,\ B2,\ B4,\ B9.
	\]
	Likewise, $|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=1$ when exactly one leg crosses the cutoff, namely on
	\[
	A2,\ A3,\ A5,\ A6,\ B3,\ B6,\ B7,\ B8,
	\]
	and $|\Delta_{p,q,k}|=2$ when both legs cross in the same direction, namely on
	\[
	B1,\ B5.
	\]
	This proves the claimed list of surviving blocks.

entries.name: rem:B9-disappears
entries.statement:
\label{rem:B9-disappears}
	The block decomposition is already useful at the level of \eqref{eq:exact-double-commutator-DQ}. In particular, the purely high--high block
	\[
	B9=(Q\to Q,\,Q\to Q)
	\]
	disappears because $\Delta_{p,q,k}=0$ there. This is crucial for the later Hilbert--Schmidt bounds: $B9$ carries no $P$-projector, hence no low-frequency input and no geometric restriction on $k$, so it would be the hardest channel to estimate directly. Every surviving block still contains at least one cutoff crossing, and therefore at least one $P$-leg from which the later bounds extract the truncated kernels $S_k^{(P)}$ and in the following we can take $s_1+s_2<1$.

entries.name: lem:aH-S
entries.statement:
[Asymmetric quartic Hilbert--Schmidt bound]\label{lem:aH-S}
	Let \(s_1,s_2\ge 0\). For a quartic kernel
	\[
	\gamma=\gamma_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2},
	\]
	define
	\[
	Q_\gamma
	:=
	\sum_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2\in\mathbb Z^2}
	\gamma_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}\,
	a_{r_1}^\ast a_{r_2}^\ast a_{u_1}a_{u_2}.
	\]
	Define the asymmetrically rescaled kernel
	\[
	\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	:=
	h(r_1)^{-s_1/2}h(r_2)^{-s_2/2}
	\gamma_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	h(u_1)^{-s_2/2}h(u_2)^{-s_1/2}.
	\]
	If \(\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\in \ell^2((\mathbb Z^2)^4)\), then for every
	\(\psi\in\mathfrak F_{\mathrm{fin}}\),
	\[
	\bigl|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle\bigr|
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}\,
	\langle \psi,(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-\mathbb{H}_{s_1+s_2})\psi\rangle
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}\,
	\langle \psi,\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\psi\rangle.
	\]
	Here $\mathfrak F_{\mathrm{fin}}$ denotes finite-particle subspace of the bosonic Fock space.
%	In particular, if \(Q_\gamma\) is self-adjoint, then as a quadratic form,
%	\[
%	\pm Q_\gamma
%	\le
%	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}\,
%	(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}+1)(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}+1).
%	\]
entries.proof:
Set
	\[
	b_u:=h(u)^{s_1/2}a_u,\qquad
	c_u:=h(u)^{s_2/2}a_u.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	a_{u_2}=h(u_2)^{-s_1/2}b_{u_2},\qquad
	a_{u_1}=h(u_1)^{-s_2/2}c_{u_1},
	\]
	and similarly
	\[
	a_{r_1}^\ast=h(r_1)^{-s_1/2}b_{r_1}^\ast,\qquad
	a_{r_2}^\ast=h(r_2)^{-s_2/2}c_{r_2}^\ast.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	Q_\gamma
	=
	\sum_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}\,
	b_{r_1}^\ast c_{r_2}^\ast c_{u_1}b_{u_2}.
	\]
	For \(\psi\in\mathfrak F_{\mathrm{fin}}\), define
	\[
	X_{u_1,u_2}:=c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi,\qquad
	Y_{r_1,r_2}:=c_{r_2}b_{r_1}\psi.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle
	=
	\sum_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\langle Y_{r_1,r_2},X_{u_1,u_2}\rangle.
	\]
	By Cauchy--Schwarz,
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|^2
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\Bigl(\sum_{r_1,r_2}\|Y_{r_1,r_2}\|^2\Bigr)
	\Bigl(\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|X_{u_1,u_2}\|^2\Bigr).
	\]
	Since the two factors are equal, it follows that
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi\|^2.
	\]
	Now
	\[
	\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi\|^2
	=
	\Bigl\langle\psi,
	\sum_{u_1,u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast c_{u_1}^\ast c_{u_1}b_{u_2}
	\,\psi\Bigr\rangle
	=
	\Bigl\langle\psi,
	\sum_{u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast \mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_{u_2}
	\,\psi\Bigr\rangle.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	[\mathbb{H}_{s_2},a_u]= -h(u)^{s_2}a_u,
	\]
	we get
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_u=b_u(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-h(u)^{s_2}).
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\sum_{u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast \mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_{u_2}
	=
	\sum_{u_2} h(u_2)^{s_1}a_{u_2}^\ast a_{u_2}(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-h(u_2)^{s_2})
	=
	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-\mathbb{H}_{s_1+s_2}.
	\]
 This proves
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\langle \psi,(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-\mathbb{H}_{s_1+s_2})\psi\rangle
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\langle \psi,\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\psi\rangle.
	\]
%	Finally,
%	\[
%	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\le (\mathbb{H}_{s_1}+1)(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}+1)
%	\]
%	as quadratic forms, so the last statement follows.

entries.name: proof_29
entries.statement:
Set
	\[
	b_u:=h(u)^{s_1/2}a_u,\qquad
	c_u:=h(u)^{s_2/2}a_u.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	a_{u_2}=h(u_2)^{-s_1/2}b_{u_2},\qquad
	a_{u_1}=h(u_1)^{-s_2/2}c_{u_1},
	\]
	and similarly
	\[
	a_{r_1}^\ast=h(r_1)^{-s_1/2}b_{r_1}^\ast,\qquad
	a_{r_2}^\ast=h(r_2)^{-s_2/2}c_{r_2}^\ast.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	Q_\gamma
	=
	\sum_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}\,
	b_{r_1}^\ast c_{r_2}^\ast c_{u_1}b_{u_2}.
	\]
	For \(\psi\in\mathfrak F_{\mathrm{fin}}\), define
	\[
	X_{u_1,u_2}:=c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi,\qquad
	Y_{r_1,r_2}:=c_{r_2}b_{r_1}\psi.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle
	=
	\sum_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}_{r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	\langle Y_{r_1,r_2},X_{u_1,u_2}\rangle.
	\]
	By Cauchy--Schwarz,
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|^2
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\Bigl(\sum_{r_1,r_2}\|Y_{r_1,r_2}\|^2\Bigr)
	\Bigl(\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|X_{u_1,u_2}\|^2\Bigr).
	\]
	Since the two factors are equal, it follows that
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi\|^2.
	\]
	Now
	\[
	\sum_{u_1,u_2}\|c_{u_1}b_{u_2}\psi\|^2
	=
	\Bigl\langle\psi,
	\sum_{u_1,u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast c_{u_1}^\ast c_{u_1}b_{u_2}
	\,\psi\Bigr\rangle
	=
	\Bigl\langle\psi,
	\sum_{u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast \mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_{u_2}
	\,\psi\Bigr\rangle.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	[\mathbb{H}_{s_2},a_u]= -h(u)^{s_2}a_u,
	\]
	we get
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_u=b_u(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-h(u)^{s_2}).
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\sum_{u_2} b_{u_2}^\ast \mathbb{H}_{s_2}b_{u_2}
	=
	\sum_{u_2} h(u_2)^{s_1}a_{u_2}^\ast a_{u_2}(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-h(u_2)^{s_2})
	=
	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-\mathbb{H}_{s_1+s_2}.
	\]
 This proves
	\[
	|\langle \psi,Q_\gamma\psi\rangle|
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\langle \psi,(\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}-\mathbb{H}_{s_1+s_2})\psi\rangle
	\le
	\|\widetilde\gamma^{(s_1,s_2)}\|_{\ell^2}
	\langle \psi,\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\psi\rangle.
	\]
%	Finally,
%	\[
%	\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\le (\mathbb{H}_{s_1}+1)(\mathbb{H}_{s_2}+1)
%	\]
%	as quadratic forms, so the last statement follows.

entries.name: lem:com1
entries.proof:
We begin with the two-cutoff contribution. It is enough to estimate \(\mathcal C_{23}\), since
	the arguments for \(\mathcal C_{56}\) and \(\mathcal C_{15}\) are identical.
	
	Write \(\mathcal C_{23}=Q_{\gamma_{23}}\) with kernel
	\[
	\gamma_{23;r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	=
	-\tilde c_\lambda\sum_{k\neq0} \rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\,
	\delta_{r_1,u_2+k}\delta_{r_2,u_1-k}\,
	P_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigl(P_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}+Q_{u_1}P_{u_1-k}\bigr).
	\]
	Let
	\(
	\widetilde\gamma_{23}:=\widetilde\gamma_{23}^{(s_1,s_2)}.
	\)
	We have
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	=
	\tilde c_\lambda^2 \sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2 A_{23}^{(p)}(k)A_{23}^{(q)}(k),
	\]
	where
	\[
	A_{23}^{(p)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_2}
	P_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\,h(u_2+k)^{-s_1}h(u_2)^{-s_1},
	\]
	\[
	A_{23}^{(q)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_1}
	\bigl(P_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}+Q_{u_1}P_{u_1-k}\bigr)
	h(u_1-k)^{-s_2}h(u_1)^{-s_2}.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	A_{23}^{(p)}(k)\le S_k^{(P)}(s_1),
	\qquad
	A_{23}^{(q)}(k)\le 2S_k^{(P)}(s_2),
	\]
	we obtain
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C\lambda^4
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2
	S_k^{(P)}(s_1)S_k^{(P)}(s_2).
	\]
	Lemma~\ref{lem:convolution-kernels} gives
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^4
	\Lambda^{4-2s_1-2s_2}
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1-2s_2}.
	\]
	{\color{red}Since $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2=\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}$ and $\beta+s_1+s_2>1$ (indeed $\beta>\frac32$ and $s_1,s_2>0$), the $k$-sum is finite, hence}
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^2\Lambda^{2-s_1-s_2}.
	\]
	Applying Lemma~\ref{lem:aH-S} yields the first bound in
	\eqref{eq:blockwise-HS-two-cutoff}; summing the identical estimates for
	\(\mathcal C_{56}\) and \(\mathcal C_{15}\) only changes the constant.
	
	\medskip
	
	For the one-cutoff pair \(\mathcal C_{36}\), write \(\mathcal C_{36}=Q_{\gamma_{36}}\) with kernel
	\[
	\gamma_{36;r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	=
	-\tilde c_\lambda\sum_{k\neq0} \rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\,
	\delta_{r_1,u_2+k}\delta_{r_2,u_1-k}\,
	\bigl(P_{u_2}Q_{u_2+k}+Q_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigr)Q_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}.
	\]
	Let \(\widetilde\gamma_{36}:=\widetilde\gamma_{36}^{(s_1,s_2)}\). As above,
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	=
	\tilde c_\lambda^2\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2 A_{36}^{(p)}(k)A_{36}^{(q)}(k),
	\]
	with
	\[
	A_{36}^{(p)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_2}
	\bigl(P_{u_2}Q_{u_2+k}+Q_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigr)
	h(u_2+k)^{-s_1}h(u_2)^{-s_1},
	\]
	\[
	A_{36}^{(q)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_1}
	Q_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}
	h(u_1-k)^{-s_2}h(u_1)^{-s_2}.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	A_{36}^{(p)}(k)\le 2S_k^{(P)}(s_1),
	\qquad
	A_{36}^{(q)}(k)\le S_k(s_2),
	\]
	we find
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C\lambda^4\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2 S_k^{(P)}(s_1)S_k(s_2).
	\]
	Now Lemma~\ref{lem:convolution-kernels} gives
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^4\Lambda^{2-2s_1}
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1},
	\]
	{\color{red}and the $k$-sum is finite because $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1}=\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta-2s_1}\in \ell^1(\mathbb Z^2)$ for every $\beta>1$. Hence}
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^2\Lambda^{1-s_1}.
	\]
	Lemma~\ref{lem:aH-S} then gives \eqref{eq:blockwise-HS-36}. The final result then follows from \eqref{dec:com}.

entries.name: proof_31
entries.statement:
We begin with the two-cutoff contribution. It is enough to estimate \(\mathcal C_{23}\), since
	the arguments for \(\mathcal C_{56}\) and \(\mathcal C_{15}\) are identical.
	
	Write \(\mathcal C_{23}=Q_{\gamma_{23}}\) with kernel
	\[
	\gamma_{23;r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	=
	-\tilde c_\lambda\sum_{k\neq0} \rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\,

	P_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigl(P_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}+Q_{u_1}P_{u_1-k}\bigr).
	\]
	Let
	\(

	We have
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2

	where

	\[
	A_{23}^{(q)}(k)

	Using
	\[
	A_{23}^{(p)}(k)\le S_k^{(P)}(s_1),

	we obtain
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C\lambda^4
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2
	S_k^{(P)}(s_1)S_k^{(P)}(s_2).
	\]
	Lemma~\ref{lem:convolution-kernels} gives
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^4
	\Lambda^{4-2s_1-2s_2}
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1-2s_2}.
	\]
	{\color{red}Since $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2=\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}$ and $\beta+s_1+s_2>1$ (indeed $\beta>\frac32$ and $s_1,s_2>0$), the $k$-sum is finite, hence}
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{23}\|_{\ell^2}
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^2\Lambda^{2-s_1-s_2}.
	\]
	Applying Lemma~\ref{lem:aH-S} yields the first bound in
	\eqref{eq:blockwise-HS-two-cutoff}; summing the identical estimates for
	\(\mathcal C_{56}\) and \(\mathcal C_{15}\) only changes the constant.
	
	\medskip
	
	For the one-cutoff pair \(\mathcal C_{36}\), write \(\mathcal C_{36}=Q_{\gamma_{36}}\) with kernel
	\[
	\gamma_{36;r_1,r_2,u_1,u_2}
	=
	-\tilde c_\lambda\sum_{k\neq0} \rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)\,
	\delta_{r_1,u_2+k}\delta_{r_2,u_1-k}\,
	\bigl(P_{u_2}Q_{u_2+k}+Q_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigr)Q_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}.
	\]
	Let \(\widetilde\gamma_{36}:=\widetilde\gamma_{36}^{(s_1,s_2)}\). As above,
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	=
	\tilde c_\lambda^2\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2 A_{36}^{(p)}(k)A_{36}^{(q)}(k),
	\]
	with
	\[
	A_{36}^{(p)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_2}
	\bigl(P_{u_2}Q_{u_2+k}+Q_{u_2}P_{u_2+k}\bigr)
	h(u_2+k)^{-s_1}h(u_2)^{-s_1},
	\]
	\[
	A_{36}^{(q)}(k)
	:=
	\sum_{u_1}
	Q_{u_1}Q_{u_1-k}
	h(u_1-k)^{-s_2}h(u_1)^{-s_2}.
	\]
	Using
	\[
	A_{36}^{(p)}(k)\le 2S_k^{(P)}(s_1),
	\qquad
	A_{36}^{(q)}(k)\le S_k(s_2),
	\]
	we find
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C\lambda^4\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2 S_k^{(P)}(s_1)S_k(s_2).
	\]
	Now Lemma~\ref{lem:convolution-kernels} gives
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}^2
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^4\Lambda^{2-2s_1}
	\sum_{k\neq0}|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1},
	\]
	{\color{red}and the $k$-sum is finite because $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2\langle k\rangle^{-2s_1}=\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta-2s_1}\in \ell^1(\mathbb Z^2)$ for every $\beta>1$. Hence}
	\[
	\|\widetilde\gamma_{36}\|_{\ell^2}
	\le
	C_{s_1,s_2}\lambda^2\Lambda^{1-s_1}.
	\]
	Lemma~\ref{lem:aH-S} then gives \eqref{eq:blockwise-HS-36}. The final result then follows from \eqref{dec:com}.

entries.name: eq:DNN-BQ
entries.statement:
[Proof of Theorem~\ref{prop:high-frequency-fluctuations}]
	We split the argument into three steps.
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1: application of the second-order correlation inequality.}
	
	Recall the self-adjoint observable
	\(
	B_Q=\frac{\lambda}{2}(\mathcal N_Q-N_{0,Q}),
	\)
	and define the perturbed Gibbs states
	\[
	\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB_Q}}
	{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}+tB_Q})},
	\qquad
	\Gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB_Q}}
	{\mathrm{Tr}(e^{-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB_Q})},
	\qquad t\in[-1,1].
	\]
	Set
	\[
	a_{Q,\lambda}:=
	\sup_{|t|\le 1}
	\left|
	\mathrm{Tr}(B_Q\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)})
	\right|.
	\]
	By the second-order correlation inequality Theorem \ref{thm:correlation-intro}, we have
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:DNN-BQ}
		\mathrm{Tr}(B_Q^2\Gamma_\lambda)
		\le
		a_{Q,\lambda}e^{a_{Q,\lambda}}
		+
		\frac14\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big([B_Q,[\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}},B_Q]]\Gamma_\lambda\Big).
	\end{equation}
Therefore
	it remains to show that both terms on the right-hand side of \eqref{eq:DNN-BQ}
	tend to zero.
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 2: the first moment of $B_Q$ under the perturbed Gibbs states is $o(1)$.}
	
	We first compare $\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}$ with the corresponding free perturbed state.
	Observe that
	\[
	-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+tB_Q
	=
	-\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h_t)-\frac{t\lambda}{2}N_{0,Q},
	\qquad
	h_t:=h-\frac{t}{2}Q.
	\]
	Hence $\Gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}$ is the quasi-free Gibbs state associated with $h_t$.
	
	For $|t|\le1$ and $\lambda$ small enough, the operator $h_t$ is uniformly comparable with $h$. We apply Proposition~\ref{prop:one-body-HS-safe} to have
%	indeed, $h_t=h$ on $PH$, while on $QH$ one has
%	\[
%	h_t \ge h-\frac12 \ge \frac12 h.
%	\]
%	The proof of Proposition~\ref{prop:one-body-HS-safe} depends only on the positivity of the
%	two-body Yukawa term, on the lower bound $h_t\ge c h$ for some $c>0$, and on the corresponding
%	free logarithmic estimates. Since these inputs are uniform for $|t|\le1$, the same argument with
%	$h$ replaced by $h_t$ yields the uniform bound
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:HS-perturbed-Q}
		\lambda\,
		\big\|
		\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)})\,\sqrt h
		\big\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
		\le
		C(1+|\log\lambda|^2),
		\qquad |t|\le1,
	\end{equation}
	where $\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}$ and $\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}$ denote the one-body density matrices of
	$\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}$ and $\Gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}$.
	
%	We have
%		\begin{equation}\label{eq:number-difference-high-safe}
%		\lambda\,
%		\left|
%		\mathrm{Tr}\big[Q(\gamma_\lambda-\gamma_0)\big]
%		\right|
%		\le
%		C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\,\|Qh^{-1}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}.
%	\end{equation}
%	Hence, if $\Lambda=\lambda^{-\alpha}$ with any $\alpha>0$, then
%	\begin{equation}\label{eq:high-number-mean-diff-safe}
%		\lambda\,
%		\left|
%		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\!\big[(\mathcal N_Q-N_{0,Q})\Gamma_\lambda\big]
%		\right|
%		\longrightarrow 0,
%		\qquad
%		\mathcal N_Q:=\mathrm d\Gamma(Q),
%		\quad
%		N_{0,Q}:=\mathrm{Tr}(Q\gamma_0).
%	\end{equation}
	By Cauchy--Schwarz in Hilbert--Schmidt norm, we obtain
	\begin{align}
		\lambda\,
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)})\big]
		\right|\le 	
			\lambda\|Qh^{-1}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
		\big\|\sqrt h\,(\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)})\,\sqrt h\big\|_{\mathrm{HS}}
		&\le
		C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\,\|Qh^{-1}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}.
		\label{eq:Q-difference-perturbed-1}
	\end{align}
	Now, in dimension $2$,
	\[
	\|Qh^{-1}\|_{\mathrm{HS}}^2
	=
	\sum_{p:\,h(p)>\Lambda^2} h(p)^{-2}
	\le C\Lambda^{-2},
	\]
	hence
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:Q-difference-perturbed-2}
		\sup_{|t|\le1}
		\lambda\,
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)})\big]
		\right|
		\le
		C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\Lambda^{-1}
		\longrightarrow 0.
	\end{equation}
	
	Next, we compare the free perturbed and unperturbed high-frequency means. Since
\[
\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}
=
\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h_t}-1},
\qquad
\gamma_0=\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h}-1},
\]
we have
\[
\lambda\,
\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_0)\big]
=
\sum_{p:\,h(p)>\Lambda^2}
\left(
\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda(h(p)-t/2)}-1}
-
\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda h(p)}-1}
\right).
\]
For each $p$ and $|t|\le 1$, the mean value theorem gives a point $\theta_{p,t}$ between $0$ and
$t$ such that
\[
\left|
\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda(h(p)-t/2)}-1}
-
\frac{\lambda}{e^{\lambda h(p)}-1}
\right|
=
\frac{|t|}{2}\,\lambda^2
\frac{e^{\lambda(h(p)-\theta_{p,t}/2)}}{\bigl(e^{\lambda(h(p)-\theta_{p,t}/2)}-1\bigr)^2}.
\]
Since $h(p)>\Lambda^2$ and $|\theta_{p,t}|\le 1$,  we have
$h(p)-\theta_{p,t}/2\ge h(p)-1/2\ge \frac12 h(p)$, and therefore
\[
\lambda^2
\frac{e^{\lambda(h(p)-\theta_{p,t}/2)}}{\bigl(e^{\lambda(h(p)-\theta_{p,t}/2)}-1\bigr)^2}
\le
C\,h(p)^{-2},
\]
because $\sup_{x>0} x^2 e^x (e^x-1)^{-2}<\infty$.
Hence
\begin{align}
	\sup_{|t|\le1}
	\lambda\,
	\left|
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_0)\big]
	\right|
	&\le
	C\sum_{p:\,h(p)>\Lambda^2} h(p)^{-2}
	\le C\Lambda^{-2}
	\longrightarrow 0.
	\label{eq:Q-difference-free-perturbed}
\end{align}
	Combining \eqref{eq:Q-difference-perturbed-2} and
	\eqref{eq:Q-difference-free-perturbed}, we find
	\begin{align}
		a_{Q,\lambda}
		&=
		\sup_{|t|\le1}
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}(B_Q\Gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)})
		\right|
		=
		\frac12
		\sup_{|t|\le1}
		\lambda\,
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}(Q\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)})-N_{0,Q}
		\right|
		\nonumber\\
		&\le
		\frac12
		\sup_{|t|\le1}
		\lambda\,
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{\lambda,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)})\big]
		\right|
		+
		\frac12
		\sup_{|t|\le1}
		\lambda\,
		\left|
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[Q(\gamma_{0,t}^{(Q)}-\gamma_0)\big]
		\right|\lesssim (1+|\log\lambda|^2)\Lambda^{-1}.
		\label{eq:aQlambda-o1}
	\end{align}
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 3: the double commutator term.}
	
	Using
	\(
	[\mathcal N_Q,\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)]=0,
	\)
we obtain
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:BQ-comm-reduction}
		[B_Q,[\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}},B_Q]]
		=
		\frac{\lambda^2}{4}\,
		[\mathcal N_Q,[W^{\mathrm{re}},\mathcal N_Q]].
	\end{equation}
	Fix $\varepsilon\in(0,\frac\alpha4)$ and choose
	\[
	s_1:=\varepsilon,
	\qquad
	s_2:=\frac12+\varepsilon,
	\qquad
	\theta:=s_1+s_2=\frac12+2\varepsilon<1.
	\]
	Then Lemma~\ref{lem:com1}, together with \eqref{eq:BQ-comm-reduction}, gives
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:BQ-comm-bound-final}
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big([B_Q,[\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}},B_Q]]\Gamma_\lambda\Big)
		\le
		C_\varepsilon\lambda^4\Lambda^{2-\theta}
		\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\Gamma_\lambda\big].
	\end{equation}
	Now Lemma~\ref{lem:holder}, in the form \eqref{eq:shell-energy-number-moment-local}, yields
	\[
	\lambda^4
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[\mathbb{H}_{s_1}\mathbb{H}_{s_2}\Gamma_\lambda\big]
	\le
	C_\varepsilon\lambda^{2-\theta}(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big([B_Q,[\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}},B_Q]]\Gamma_\lambda\Big)
	\le
	C_\varepsilon\lambda^{2-\theta}\Lambda^{2-\theta}(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	Since \(\theta=\frac12+2\varepsilon\) and \(\varepsilon\) small enough,
	\[
	\lambda^{2-\theta}\Lambda^{2-\theta}
	=
	\lambda^{(2-\theta)(1-\alpha)}
	\le
	\lambda^{3/2-2\alpha}
	=
	\lambda^{3/2}\Lambda^2.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big([B_Q,[\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}},B_Q]]\Gamma_\lambda\Big)
	\le
	C\lambda^{\frac32}\Lambda^2(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	Since \eqref{eq:aQlambda-o1} implies $a_{Q,\lambda}\to0$, we have $a_{Q,\lambda}e^{a_{Q,\lambda}}\le
	2a_{Q,\lambda}$ for small $\lambda$. Inserting this and the last estimate into
	\eqref{eq:DNN-BQ} yields \eqref{eq:high-frequency-fluctuation-bound}.

entries.name: rem:HF0-main-input
entries.statement:
%	\label{rem:HF0-main-input}
%	This is the only genuinely new input in the $k=0$ channel. Once
%	\eqref{eq:high-frequency-fluctuation-bound} is available, the control of $HF_0(\Gamma_\lambda)$ is
%	immediate. A natural route is to localize the relative entropy to $\mathfrak F(QH)$ and study the
%	perturbed partition functions associated with $\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}} + t \lambda \mathcal N_Q$.
%
entries.proof:
[Proof of Theorem \ref{cor:HF0-vanishes}]
	Set
	\[
	A_\lambda:=\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[(\mathcal N_Q-N_{0,Q})^2\Gamma_\lambda\big],
	\qquad
	B_\lambda:=\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}\!\big[(\mathcal N_P-N_{0,P})^2\Gamma_\lambda\big].
	\]
	By Cauchy--Schwarz,
	\[
	|HF_0(\Gamma_\lambda)|
	\le
	CA_\lambda
	+
	C B_\lambda^{1/2}A_\lambda^{1/2}.
	\]
	Using Theorem~\ref{prop:high-frequency-fluctuations} with $\Lambda=\lambda^{-\alpha}$, we obtain
	\[
	A_\lambda
	\le
	C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\bigl(\Lambda^{-1}+\lambda^{3/2}\Lambda^2\bigr)
	=
	C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\bigl(\lambda^\alpha+\lambda^{3/2-2\alpha}\bigr).
	\]
	On the other hand, we have
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:low-number-fluctuation-log2}B_\lambda
		\le C(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\end{equation}
	In fact, we have
	\[
	(\mathcal N_P-N_{0,P})^2\le 2\mathcal N_P^2+2N_{0,P}^2.
	\]
	For the first term, the operator inequality $\mathcal N_P\le\mathcal N$ gives
	\[
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F(H)}(\mathcal N_P^2\Gamma_{\lambda})
	\le
	\lambda^2\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F(H)}(\mathcal N^2\Gamma_\lambda)
	\le C(1+|\log\lambda|^2),
	\]
	by Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log}. For the second term, $0\le P\le1$ implies
	\[
	\lambda^2N_{0,P}^2\le(\lambda N_0)^2\le C(1+|\log\lambda|^2).
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:low-number-fluctuation-log2}. Therefore
	\[
	|HF_0(\Gamma_\lambda)|
	\le
	C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\bigl(\lambda^\alpha+\lambda^{3/2-2\alpha}\bigr)
	+
	C(1+|\log\lambda|^2)\bigl(\lambda^{\alpha/2}+\lambda^{3/4-\alpha}\bigr).
	\]
	Since $0<\alpha<\frac34$, one has $\lambda^\alpha\le \lambda^{\alpha/2}$ and
	$\lambda^{3/2-2\alpha}\le \lambda^{3/4-\alpha}$ for $0<\lambda<1$. Hence
\eqref{eq:HF0-vanishes-rate} and the convergence
	\eqref{eq:HF0-vanishes} follow immediately.

entries.statement:

entries.name: prop:shell-vanishing-local
lem:shell-decomposition-local
entries.proof:
We expand the quadratic part first and isolate the one-body remainder afterwards.
	
%	\smallskip
%	\noindent\textbf{Step 1. Expanding the compressed two-body operator.}
	Using the Fourier representation
	\[
	V_{\neq0}
	=
	\sum_{\ell\neq 0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(\ell)
	(M_{e_\ell}\otimes M_{e_{-\ell}})
	\]
	and the bosonic quantization identity
	\[
	\mathbb{W}(X\otimes Y)=\frac12\bigl(\mathrm d\Gamma(X)\mathrm d\Gamma(Y)-\mathrm d\Gamma(XY)\bigr),
	\]
	we obtain
	\[
	\mathbb{W}(\Pi_1V_{\neq0}\Pi_1)
	=
	\frac{1}{2}
	\sum_{\ell\neq0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(\ell)
	\Big(
	\mathrm d\Gamma(Se_\ell S)\,\mathrm d\Gamma(Se_{-\ell}S)
	-
	\mathrm d\Gamma(Se_\ell Se_{-\ell}S)
	\Big),
	\]
	and the same formula with $S$ replaced by $P$ for $\Pi V_{\neq0}\Pi$.
	
%	\smallskip
%	\noindent\textbf{Step 2. Identifying the shell contributions.}
	Since
	\[
	\mathrm d\Gamma(Se_\ell S)=A_\ell+B_\ell,
	\]
	the quadratic difference is exactly
	\[
	(A_\ell+B_\ell)(A_{-\ell}+B_{-\ell})-A_\ell A_{-\ell}
	=
	A_\ell B_{-\ell}+B_\ell A_{-\ell}+B_\ell B_{-\ell}.
	\]
	This is the quadratic part appearing in \eqref{eq:shell-decomposition-local}. The leftover
	one-body contribution is therefore
	\[
	R_{\rm shell}^{(1)}(\Gamma)
	=
	-\frac{\lambda^2}{4}
	\sum_{\ell\neq0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(\ell)
	\mathrm{Tr}\!\Big(\mathrm d\Gamma(Se_\ell Se_{-\ell}S-Pe_\ell Pe_{-\ell}P)\Gamma\Big).
	\]
	
	We then bound the one-body remainder.
	Introduce
	\[
	K_{\rm shell}
	:=
	\sum_{\ell\neq0}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(\ell)
	\bigl(Se_\ell Se_{-\ell}S-Pe_\ell Pe_{-\ell}P\bigr).
	\]
	{#1{This operator is diagonal in the Fourier basis, and its symbol is bounded by
	\[
	\sup_p \sum_{q:\,h(q)\leq \Lambda_1^2}\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(q-p)
	\le C_\beta(1+\log\Lambda_1)\Lambda_1^{2-\beta}.
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\|K_{\rm shell}\|\le C_\beta(1+\log\Lambda_1)\Lambda_1^{2-\beta}.
	\]
	It follows that
	\[
	|R_{\rm shell}^{(1)}(\Gamma)|
	\le C_\beta\lambda^2(1+\log\Lambda_1)\Lambda_1^{2-\beta}\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N\Gamma).
	\]
	which by Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log-safe} gives
	\eqref{eq:shell-remainder-local}.}}

entries.statement:

	\]
