	\]
	in dimension two. Thus
	\[
	\|\lambda^m\Gamma_{0,Q}^{(m)}\|_{\mathfrak S^2}
	\le

	\le
	C_m\Lambda^{-1},
	\]
	which proves
	\(
	\|\lambda^m\Gamma_{0,Q}^{(m)}\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\le C_m\lambda^\alpha
	\)
	for every fixed $m\ge1$. Therefore all terms with at least one high-frequency free factor are bounded by $C_k\lambda^\alpha(1+|\log\lambda|)^k$, and summing over the finitely many values of $\ell$ proves \eqref{eq:HS-hat-to-low-frequency}.
	
	Combining \eqref{eq:HS-hat-to-low-frequency} with Proposition~\ref{prop:HS-state-to-rdm-scaled} yields \eqref{eq:HS-true-to-low-frequency}.

entries.name: prop:HS-deFinetti-remainder-vanish
entries.proof:
Apply the quantitative de Finetti estimate \eqref{eq:quantitative} from Theorem~\ref{thm:quant deF} with $\Gamma=\Gamma_{\lambda,P}$. This yields
	\begin{align*}
		&\|k!\lambda^k\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(k)}-M_{\lambda,P}^{(k)}\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\lambda^k
		\sum_{\ell=0}^{k-1}
		\binom{k}{\ell}^2
		\frac{(k-\ell+K-1)!}{(K-1)!}
		\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_{\lambda,P}).
	\end{align*}
	Since
	\[
	\frac{(k-\ell+K-1)!}{(K-1)!}\le C_k K^{k-\ell},
	\qquad
	\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_{\lambda,P})\le \mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda),
	\]
	and Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} gives
	\(
	\lambda^\ell\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda)\le C_\ell(1+|\log\lambda|)^\ell
	\),
	we obtain \eqref{eq:HS-deFinetti-remainder-trace}. The Hilbert--Schmidt bound follows from
	\(
	\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\le \|A\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
	\).
	Finally,
	\[
	\lambda K\sim \lambda\Lambda^2=\lambda^{1-2\alpha}\to0
	\qquad (0<\alpha<1/4),
	\]
	so the right-hand side tends to $0$.

entries.name: proof_117
entries.statement:
Apply the quantitative de Finetti estimate \eqref{eq:quantitative} from Theorem~\ref{thm:quant deF} with $\Gamma=\Gamma_{\lambda,P}$. This yields
	\begin{align*}
		&\|k!\lambda^k\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(k)}-M_{\lambda,P}^{(k)}\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\lambda^k
		\sum_{\ell=0}^{k-1}
		\binom{k}{\ell}^2
		\frac{(k-\ell+K-1)!}{(K-1)!}
		\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_{\lambda,P}).
	\end{align*}
	Since
	\[
	\frac{(k-\ell+K-1)!}{(K-1)!}\le C_k K^{k-\ell},
	\qquad
	\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_{\lambda,P})\le \mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda),
	\]
	and Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} gives
	\(
	\lambda^\ell\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda)\le C_\ell(1+|\log\lambda|)^\ell
	\),
	we obtain \eqref{eq:HS-deFinetti-remainder-trace}. The Hilbert--Schmidt bound follows from
	\(
	\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\le \|A\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
	\).
	Finally,
	\[
	\lambda K\sim \lambda\Lambda^2=\lambda^{1-2\alpha}\to0
	\qquad (0<\alpha<1/4),
	\]
	so the right-hand side tends to $0$.

entries.name: lem:HS-full-norm-moment-mu
entries.proof:
By definition of $M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}$,
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr} M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	=
	\int_{PH}\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(|u^{\otimes m}\rangle\langle u^{\otimes m}|\bigr)\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u)
	=
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u).
	\]
	On the other hand, Theorem~\ref{thm:quant deF} implies
	\[
	M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	=
	m!\lambda^m\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}+R_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	\]
	with
	\[
	\|R_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
	\le
	C_m
	\sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}
	(\lambda K)^{m-\ell}\lambda^\ell\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda)
	\le C_m.
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u)
	\le
	m!\lambda^m\mathrm{Tr}(\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(m)})+C_m
	\le
	m!\lambda^m\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^m\Gamma_\lambda)+C_m.
	\]
	Applying Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} completes the proof.

entries.name: proof_119
entries.statement:
By definition of $M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}$,
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr} M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	=
	\int_{PH}\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(|u^{\otimes m}\rangle\langle u^{\otimes m}|\bigr)\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u)
	=
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u).
	\]
	On the other hand, Theorem~\ref{thm:quant deF} implies
	\[
	M_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	=
	m!\lambda^m\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}+R_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}
	\]
	with
	\[
	\|R_{\lambda,P}^{(m)}\|_{\mathfrak S^1}
	\le
	C_m
	\sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}
	(\lambda K)^{m-\ell}\lambda^\ell\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^\ell\Gamma_\lambda)
	\le C_m.
	\]
	Therefore
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}(u)
	\le
	m!\lambda^m\mathrm{Tr}(\Gamma_{\lambda,P}^{(m)})+C_m
	\le
	m!\lambda^m\mathrm{Tr}(\mathcal N^m\Gamma_\lambda)+C_m.
	\]
	Applying Proposition~\ref{prop:number-moments-log} completes the proof.

entries.name: lem:HS-full-norm-moment-nuP
entries.proof:
By definition,
	\[
	\,\mathrm d\nu_P(u)=z_P^{-1}e^{-D_P(u)}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u),
	\qquad
	z_P:=\int_{PH}e^{-D_P(u)}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u).
	\]
	Since $D_P\ge0$, we have $0\le e^{-D_P}\le1$. Moreover, Lemma \ref{lem:class} implies
	\(
	z_P\to z_{\rm cl}>0
	\),
	so there exists $c_0>0$ such that $z_P\ge c_0$ for all sufficiently small $\lambda$. Hence
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\nu_P(u)
	\le
	c_0^{-1}\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u).
	\]
	The right-hand side is the $2m$-th moment of a centered complex Gaussian with covariance $Ph^{-1}P$. By Wick's formula,
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u)
	\le
	{\color{red}C_m\bigl(\mathrm{Tr}(Ph^{-1}P)\bigr)^m
	\le C_m(1+\log\Lambda)^m
	\le C_m(1+|\log\lambda|)^m.}
	\]
{\color{red}This implies \eqref{eq:HS-full-norm-moment-nuP}.}

entries.name: proof_121
entries.statement:
By definition,
	\[
	\,\mathrm d\nu_P(u)=z_P^{-1}e^{-D_P(u)}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u),
	\qquad
	z_P:=\int_{PH}e^{-D_P(u)}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u).
	\]
	Since $D_P\ge0$, we have $0\le e^{-D_P}\le1$. Moreover, Lemma \ref{lem:class} implies
	\(
	z_P\to z_{\rm cl}>0
	\),
	so there exists $c_0>0$ such that $z_P\ge c_0$ for all sufficiently small $\lambda$. Hence
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\nu_P(u)
	\le
	c_0^{-1}\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u).
	\]
	The right-hand side is the $2m$-th moment of a centered complex Gaussian with covariance $Ph^{-1}P$. By Wick's formula,
	\[
	\int_{PH}\|u\|^{2m}\,\,\mathrm d\mu_{0,P}(u)
	\le
	{\color{red}C_m\bigl(\mathrm{Tr}(Ph^{-1}P)\bigr)^m
	\le C_m(1+\log\Lambda)^m
	\le C_m(1+|\log\lambda|)^m.}
	\]
{\color{red}This implies \eqref{eq:HS-full-norm-moment-nuP}.}

entries.name: lem:HS-L1-to-moment-operator
entries.proof:
By duality for Hilbert--Schmidt operators,
	\[
	\|M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)}\|_{\mathfrak S^2}
	=
	\sup_{\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\le1}
	\Bigl|
	\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
	\Bigr|.
	\]
	For such an $A$,
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
	=
	\int_E \langle u^{\otimes k},Au^{\otimes k}\rangle\,\,\mathrm d(\mu-\eta)(u).
	\]
	Moreover,
	\[
	|\langle u^{\otimes k},Au^{\otimes k}\rangle|
	\le
	\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\|u^{\otimes k}\|^2
	\le
	\|u\|^{2k}.
	\]
	Hence Cauchy--Schwarz with respect to the positive measure $|\,\mathrm d\mu-\,\mathrm d\eta|$ gives
	\begin{align*}
		&\Bigl|
		\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
		\Bigr|
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\Bigl(
		\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,|\,\mathrm d-\,\mathrm d|(u)
		\Bigr)^{1/2}
		\|\mu-\eta\|_{L^1(E)}^{1/2}
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\Bigl(
		\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,\,\mathrm d(u)+\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,\,\mathrm d(u)
		\Bigr)^{1/2}
		\|\mu-\eta\|_{L^1(E)}^{1/2}.
	\end{align*}
	Taking the supremum over $A$ proves \eqref{eq:HS-L1-to-moment-operator}.

entries.name: proof_123
entries.statement:
By duality for Hilbert--Schmidt operators,
	\[
	\|M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)}\|_{\mathfrak S^2}
	=
	\sup_{\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\le1}
	\Bigl|
	\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
	\Bigr|.
	\]
	For such an $A$,
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
	=
	\int_E \langle u^{\otimes k},Au^{\otimes k}\rangle\,\,\mathrm d(\mu-\eta)(u).
	\]
	Moreover,
	\[
	|\langle u^{\otimes k},Au^{\otimes k}\rangle|
	\le
	\|A\|_{\mathfrak S^2}\|u^{\otimes k}\|^2
	\le
	\|u\|^{2k}.
	\]
	Hence Cauchy--Schwarz with respect to the positive measure $|\,\mathrm d\mu-\,\mathrm d\eta|$ gives
	\begin{align*}
		&\Bigl|
		\mathrm{Tr}\bigl(A(M_\mu^{(k)}-M_\eta^{(k)})\bigr)
		\Bigr|
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\Bigl(
		\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,|\,\mathrm d-\,\mathrm d|(u)
		\Bigr)^{1/2}
		\|\mu-\eta\|_{L^1(E)}^{1/2}
		\\
		&\qquad\le
		\Bigl(
		\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,\,\mathrm d(u)+\int_E\|u\|^{4k}\,\,\mathrm d(u)
		\Bigr)^{1/2}
		\|\mu-\eta\|_{L^1(E)}^{1/2}.
	\end{align*}
	Taking the supremum over $A$ proves \eqref{eq:HS-L1-to-moment-operator}.

entries.name: prop:HS-low-frequency-to-fd-classical
entries.proof:
Apply Lemma~\ref{lem:HS-L1-to-moment-operator} with
	\(
	\mu=\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}
	\)
	and
	\(
	\eta=\nu_P
	\).
	Using Lemmas~\ref{lem:HS-full-norm-moment-mu} and \ref{lem:HS-full-norm-moment-nuP}, we obtain
	\[
	\|M_{\lambda,P}^{(k)}-\gamma_{\nu_P}^{(k)}\|_{\mathfrak S^2}
	\le
	C_k(1+|\log\lambda|)^k
	\|\mu^\lambda_{P,\lambda}-\nu_P\|_{L^1(PH)}^{1/2}.
	\]
	By Proposition~\ref{prop:weighted-lower-symbol},
we obtain \eqref{eq:HS-M-lambdaP-to-gamma-nuP}.
	Combining \eqref{eq:HS-M-lambdaP-to-gamma-nuP} with Proposition~\ref{prop:HS-deFinetti-remainder-vanish} yields \eqref{eq:HS-low-frequency-to-fd-classical-main}.

entries.name: proof_126
entries.statement:
[Proof of Theorem~\ref{thm:HS-convergence-main}]
	Fix $k\ge1$.
	By Proposition~\ref{prop:HS-hat-to-low-frequency},
 Proposition~\ref{prop:HS-low-frequency-to-fd-classical} and Proposition~\ref{prop:HS-classical-cutoff-limit} the result follows.

entries.name: lem:periodic-yukawa-positive
entries.proof:
{#1{Write the periodic fractional-Bessel kernel by subordination as}}
	\[
	{\rmk{v_\beta}(x)=\frac1{\Gamma(\beta/2)}\int_0^\infty t^{\beta/2-1}e^{-t}p_t(x)\,\,\mathrm d t,}
	\]
	where $p_t$ is the heat kernel on $\mathbb T^2$:
	\[
	p_t(x)
	=
	\frac1{(2\pi)^2}\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z^2}e^{-t|k|^2}e^{\mathrm i k\cdot x}
	=
	\sum_{n\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac{1}{4\pi t}
	\exp\!\left(-\frac{|x+2\pi n|^2}{4t}\right).
	\]
	Fix $x\in\mathbb T^2$, viewed as a representative in $[-\pi,\pi]^2$. Then $|x|^2\le 2\pi^2$. Since every term in the periodized heat kernel is nonnegative, we have
	\[
	p_t(x)
	\ge
	\frac{1}{4\pi t}\exp\!\left(-\frac{|x|^2}{4t}\right)
	\ge
	\frac{e^{-\pi^2/2}}{4\pi t}
	\qquad (1\le t\le 2).
	\]
	Hence
	\[
	\rmk{v_\beta}(x)
	\ge
	{\frac1{\Gamma(\beta/2)}\int_1^2 t^{\beta/2-1}e^{-t}p_t(x)\,\,\mathrm d t}
	\ge
	{\frac{e^{-2-\pi^2/2}}{4\pi\Gamma(\beta/2)}\int_1^2 t^{\beta/2-2}\,\,\mathrm d t>0.}
	\]
	This proves the claim.

entries.name: prop:def-Gibbs
entries.statement:
[Existence of the grand-canonical Gibbs state]\label{prop:def-Gibbs}
	For every fixed $\lambda>0$, the formal Hamiltonian
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	=
	\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+W^{\mathrm{re}}
	\]
defined in \eqref{eq:Hre}	admits a natural realization as a self-adjoint operator on bosonic Fock space,
	which is bounded from below. Moreover,
	\[
	Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	:=
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)<\infty.
	\]
	Hence the grand-canonical Gibbs state
	\[
	\Gamma_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	:=
	\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}}{Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}
	\]
	is well defined.
entries.proof:
We divide the proof into four steps.
	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 1: sector-wise realization.}
	Recall that
	\[
	\mathfrak F=\bigoplus_{n\ge0}\mathfrak H_n,
	\qquad
	\mathfrak H_n=L^2_s((\mathbb T^2)^n).
	\]
 Therefore the restriction of the formal Hamiltonian to $\mathfrak H_n$ is
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:Hn-re}
		\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}
		=
		\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j+1)
		+
		\lambda^2\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}#1{v_\beta}(x_i-x_j)
		+
		c_\lambda n + C_\lambda.
	\end{equation}
	
	We define $\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}$ rigorously through its quadratic form
	\begin{align*}
		q_{\lambda,n}[\Psi]
		:={}&
		\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n\|\nabla_j\Psi\|_{L^2((\mathbb T^2)^n)}^2
		+
		((\lambda+c_\lambda)n+C_\lambda)\|\Psi\|_{L^2((\mathbb T^2)^n)}^2
		\\
		&\quad
		+
		\lambda^2\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}
		\int_{(\mathbb T^2)^n}#1{v_\beta}(x_i-x_j)|\Psi(X_n)|^2\,\,\mathrm d_n,
	\end{align*}
	with form domain
	\[
	Q_{\lambda,n}
	=
	H^1_s((\mathbb T^2)^n)
	\cap
	\left\{
	\Psi\in L^2_s((\mathbb T^2)^n):
	\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}\int \rmk{v_\beta}(x_i-x_j)|\Psi|^2<\infty
	\right\}.
	\]
	Here $X_n=(x_1,\dots,x_n)$ and $\,\mathrm d X_n=\,\mathrm d x_1\cdots \,\mathrm d x_n$.
	
	Since the potential term is nonnegative, the form $q_{\lambda,n}$ is densely defined and bounded from below on each fixed sector. The set
	\[
	C^\infty\!\big((\mathbb T^2)^n\setminus\mathcal D_n\big)\cap \mathfrak H_n,
	\qquad
	\mathcal D_n:=\{X_n:\ x_i=x_j\ \text{for some }i\neq j\},
	\]
	is dense in $\mathfrak H_n$ because $\mathcal D_n$ has measure zero. The potential form
	\[
	\Psi\mapsto
	\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}\int \rmk{v_\beta}(x_i-x_j)|\Psi|^2
	\]
	is closed as a nonnegative multiplication form. Hence $q_{\lambda,n}$ is closed and bounded below, so by the Friedrichs construction it determines a unique self-adjoint operator on $\mathfrak H_n$, still denoted by $\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}$.
	
	Finally, define
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	:=
	\bigoplus_{n\ge0}\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}
	\]
	on Fock space. This gives a precise meaning to the formal expression
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}=\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(h)+\rmk{W_{\beta}^{(2)}}+c_\lambda \mathcal N+C_\lambda.
	\]
	By Lemma~\ref{lem:periodic-yukawa-positive}, there exists $\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}>0$ such that
	\[
	\rmk{v_\beta}(x)\ge \rmk{v_{\beta,*}}
	\qquad\text{for all }x\in\mathbb T^2\setminus\{0\}.
	\]
	Consequently, for almost every $X_n\in(\mathbb T^2)^n$,
	\[
	\sum_{1\le i<j\le n}\rmk{v_\beta}(x_i-x_j)
	\ge
	\frac{\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{2}n(n-1).
	\]

	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 2: coercive lower bound in the particle number.}
	Using \eqref{eq:Hn-re} and the previous step, we obtain on $\mathfrak H_n$
	\begin{align*}
		\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}
		&\ge
		\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)
		+
		\lambda n
		+
		\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{2}n(n-1)
		+
		c_\lambda n
		+
		C_\lambda
		\\
		&=
		\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)
		+
		a_\lambda n^2
		+
		b_\lambda n
		+
		C_\lambda,
	\end{align*}
	where
	\[
	a_\lambda:=\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{2}>0,
	\qquad
	b_\lambda:=\lambda+c_\lambda-a_\lambda.
	\]
	Now
	\[
	a_\lambda n^2+b_\lambda n
	\ge
	\frac{a_\lambda}{2}n^2-\frac{b_\lambda^2}{2a_\lambda}
	=
	\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2-\frac{b_\lambda^2}{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}.
	\]
yields
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}
	\ge
	\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)
	+
	\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2
	-
	C_\lambda',
	\]
	where we may take
	\[
	C_\lambda':=\frac{b_\lambda^2}{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}+|C_\lambda|.
	\]
	Therefore, on Fock space,
	\begin{equation}\label{eq:global-lower-bound}
		\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
		\ge
		\lambda \mathrm d\Gamma(-\Delta)
		+
		\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}\mathcal N^2
		-
		C_\lambda'.
	\end{equation}

	
	\medskip
	
	\noindent\textbf{Step 3: trace-class bound.}
	For fixed $n\ge0$, set
	\[
	K_{\lambda,n}
	:=
	\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)
	+
	\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2
	-
	C_\lambda'.
	\]
	By \eqref{eq:global-lower-bound},
	\[
	\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}\ge K_{\lambda,n}
	\qquad\text{on }\mathfrak H_n.
	\]
%	Moreover, both $K_{\lambda,n}$ and $\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}$ have compact resolvent on
%	$\mathfrak H_n$: for $K_{\lambda,n}$ this is immediate from the torus Laplacian, while for
%	$\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}$ it follows from the compact embedding
%	$H^1((\mathbb T^2)^n)\hookrightarrow L^2((\mathbb T^2)^n)$ together with the form construction in Step~1.
%	Hence the min--max principle yields
%	\[
%	\mu_j\big(\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}\big)
%	\ge
%	\mu_j(K_{\lambda,n})
%	\qquad\text{for every }j\ge1,
%	\]
%	where $\mu_j(\cdot)$ denotes the $j$-th eigenvalue counted with multiplicity.
	Therefore
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
	\leq
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-K_{\lambda,n}}\big)
		=
		e^{C_\lambda'}e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\left(
		e^{-\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)}
		\right).
	\end{align*}
	
	Now set
	\[
	A_\lambda
	:=
	\mathrm{Tr}_{L^2(\mathbb T^2)}\big(e^{-\lambda(-\Delta)}\big)
	=
	\sum_{k\in\mathbb Z^2}e^{-\lambda|k|^2}
	<\infty.
	\]
	Since $\mathfrak H_n$ is a subspace of the full tensor product $\bigotimes^nL^2(\mathbb T^2)$ and the operator is positive,
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\left(
		e^{-\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)}
		\right)
		&\le
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\otimes^nL^2(\mathbb T^2)}\left(
		(e^{-\lambda(-\Delta)})^{\otimes n}
		\right)
		\\
		&=
		\big(\mathrm{Tr}_{L^2(\mathbb T^2)}e^{-\lambda(-\Delta)}\big)^n
		=
		A_\lambda^n.
	\end{align*}
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
	\le
	e^{C_\lambda'}e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}A_\lambda^n.
	\]
	Summing over all particle numbers yields
	\begin{align*}
		Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
		=
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
		&=
		\sum_{n\ge0}\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
		\\
		&\le
		e^{C_\lambda'}\sum_{n\ge0}e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}A_\lambda^n
		<\infty.
	\end{align*}
	Since the Gaussian factor $e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}$ dominates the exponential growth $A_\lambda^n$ as $n\to\infty$, we conclude that $e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}$ is trace class. Therefore the Gibbs state
	\[
	\Gamma_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	=
	\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}}{\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}})}
	\]
	is well defined.

	<\infty.
	\]
	Since $\mathfrak H_n$ is a subspace of the full tensor product $\bigotimes^nL^2(\mathbb T^2)$ and the operator is positive,
	\begin{align*}
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\left(
		e^{-\lambda\sum_{j=1}^n(-\Delta_j)}
		\right)
		&\le
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\otimes^nL^2(\mathbb T^2)}\left(
		(e^{-\lambda(-\Delta)})^{\otimes n}
		\right)
		\\
		&=
		\big(\mathrm{Tr}_{L^2(\mathbb T^2)}e^{-\lambda(-\Delta)}\big)^n
		=
		A_\lambda^n.
	\end{align*}
	Hence
	\[
	\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
	\le
	e^{C_\lambda'}e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}A_\lambda^n.
	\]
	Summing over all particle numbers yields
	\begin{align*}
		Z_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
		=
		\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
		&=
		\sum_{n\ge0}\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak H_n}\big(e^{-\mathbb{H}_{\lambda,n}^{\mathrm{re}}}\big)
		\\
		&\le
		e^{C_\lambda'}\sum_{n\ge0}e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}A_\lambda^n
		<\infty.
	\end{align*}
	Since the Gaussian factor $e^{-\frac{\lambda^2\rmk{v_{\beta,*}}}{4}n^2}$ dominates the exponential growth $A_\lambda^n$ as $n\to\infty$, we conclude that $e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}$ is trace class. Therefore the Gibbs state
	\[
	\Gamma_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}
	=
	\frac{e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}}}{\mathrm{Tr}_{\mathfrak F}(e^{-\mathbb{H}_\lambda^{\mathrm{re}}})}
	\]
	is well defined.

entries.name: lem:sum
entries.proof:
Set
	\[
	A_2(k):=
	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}.
	\]
	If $|k|\le 2$, then
	\[
	A_2(k)
	\le
	\sum_{p\in\mathbb Z^2}(|p|^2+1)^{-2}
	\le C.
	\]
	Assume now that $|k|>2$, and set $R:=|k|/2$. Decompose
	\[
	\mathbb Z^2=\Omega_1\cup\Omega_2\cup\Omega_3\cup\Omega_4,
	\]
	where
	\[
	\Omega_1:=\{p\in\mathbb Z^2:\ |p|\le R\},
	\qquad
	\Omega_2:=\{p\in\mathbb Z^2:\ |p+k|\le R\},
	\]
	\[
	\Omega_3:=\{p\in\mathbb Z^2:\ |p|>R,\ |p+k|>R,\ |p|\le 2|k|\},
	\qquad
	\Omega_4:=\{p\in\mathbb Z^2:\ |p|>2|k|\}.
	\]
	These four sets cover $\mathbb Z^2$.
	
	On $\Omega_1$ we have $|p+k|\ge |k|-|p|\ge R$, and therefore
	\[
	\sum_{p\in\Omega_1}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
	\le
	\frac{C}{1+|k|^2}\sum_{|p|\le R}\frac1{|p|^2+1}
	\le
	C\,\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	By the change of variables $q=p+k$, the same estimate holds on $\Omega_2$:
	\[
	\sum_{p\in\Omega_2}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
	\le
	C\,\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	
	On $\Omega_3$ we still have $|p+k|>R$, hence
	\[
	\sum_{p\in\Omega_3}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
	\le
	\frac{C}{1+|k|^2}\sum_{R<|p|\le 2|k|}\frac1{|p|^2+1}
	\le
	C\,\frac{\log(2+|k|)}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	
	Finally, if $p\in\Omega_4$, then $|p+k|\ge |p|-|k|\ge |p|/2$, and therefore
	\[
	\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
	\le
	\frac{C}{(1+|p|^2)^2}.
	\]
	Since
	\[
	\sum_{|p|>2|k|}(1+|p|^2)^{-2}
	\le
	\frac{C}{1+|k|^2}
	\qquad\text{in dimension }2,
	\]
	we obtain
	\[
	\sum_{p\in\Omega_4}\frac1{(|p+k|^2+1)(|p|^2+1)}
	\le
	\frac{C}{1+|k|^2}.
	\]
	Combining the four regions proves \eqref{eq:sum-log}.

entries.name: lem:convolution-kernels
entries.statement:
[Convolution kernels]\label{lem:convolution-kernels}
	For \(s>0\), define
	\[
	S_k(s):=\sum_{u\in\mathbb Z^2} h(u+k)^{-s}h(u)^{-s}.
	\]
	For $L\ge1$, let
	\[
	\chi_u^{(L)}:=\mathbf 1_{\{h(u)\le L^2\}},
	\qquad
	S_k^{(L)}(s):=\sum_{u\in\mathbb Z^2} \chi_u^{(L)}\,h(u+k)^{-s}h(u)^{-s}.
	\]
	Then the following hold.
	\begin{align}
		S_k^{(L)}(s)&\le C_sL^{2-2s}\langle k\rangle^{-2s},
		&&0<s<1,\quad L\ge1,
		\label{eq:SkL-bound}
		\\
		S_k(s)&\le C_s,
		&&s>\frac12.
		\label{eq:Sk-bound}
	\end{align}
	In particular,
	\begin{align}
		S_k^{(P)}(s)&\le C_s\Lambda^{2-2s}\langle k\rangle^{-2s},
		&&0<s<1,
		\label{eq:SkP-bound}
		\\
		S_k^{(S)}(s)&\le C_s\Lambda_1^{2-2s}\langle k\rangle^{-2s},
		&&0<s<1.
		\label{eq:SkS-bound}
	\end{align}
entries.proof:
Since $h(u)\asymp \langle u\rangle^2$, \eqref{eq:Sk-bound} follows from \eqref{eq:sum-general}
	with $d=2$ and $l=m=2s$.
	
	For \eqref{eq:SkL-bound}, note first that
	\[
	|k|\le |u|+|u+k|,
	\]
	so at least one of $|u|$ and $|u+k|$ is bounded below by $|k|/2$. Consequently,
	\[
	h(u+k)^{-s}h(u)^{-s}
	\le
	C_s\langle k\rangle^{-2s}\bigl(h(u)^{-s}+h(u+k)^{-s}\bigr).
	\]
	Summing over the truncated region gives
	\[
	S_k^{(L)}(s)
	\le
	C_s\langle k\rangle^{-2s}
	\sum_{u\in\mathbb Z^2}\chi_u^{(L)}\bigl(h(u)^{-s}+h(u+k)^{-s}\bigr).
	\]
	We estimate the two sums on the right separately.
	
	Since $\chi_u^{(L)}=1$ implies $h(u)\le L^2$ and hence $|u|\lesssim L$, the standard lattice
	bound yields
	\[
	\sum_{u\in\mathbb Z^2}\chi_u^{(L)} h(u)^{-s}
	\le
	C_s\sum_{|u|\lesssim L}\langle u\rangle^{-2s}
	\le C_sL^{2-2s},
	\qquad 0<s<1.
	\]
	For the shifted term, change variables $v=u+k$:
	\[
	\sum_{u\in\mathbb Z^2}\chi_u^{(L)} h(u+k)^{-s}
	=
	\sum_{v\in\mathbb Z^2}\chi_{v-k}^{(L)} h(v)^{-s}.
	\]
	The support of $\chi_{v-k}^{(L)}$ is contained in a lattice ball $B(k,CL)$ of radius $CL$.
	For $j\ge0$, let
	\[
	\mathcal A_j:=\{v\in\mathbb Z^2:\ 2^j\le \langle v\rangle<2^{j+1}\}.
	\]
	Then
	\[
	\#\bigl(B(k,CL)\cap \mathcal A_j\bigr)
	\le
	C\min\{L^2,2^{2j}\}.
	\]
	Therefore
	\begin{align*}
		\sum_{v\in\mathbb Z^2}\chi_{v-k}^{(L)} h(v)^{-s}
		&\le
		C_s\sum_{j\ge0}2^{-2sj}\,\#\bigl(B(k,CL)\cap \mathcal A_j\bigr)
		\\
		&\le
		C_s\sum_{j\ge0}2^{-2sj}\min\{L^2,2^{2j}\}
		\\
		&\le
		C_s\Bigl(\sum_{2^j\le L}2^{(2-2s)j}+L^2\sum_{2^j>L}2^{-2sj}\Bigr)
		\le C_sL^{2-2s}.
	\end{align*}
	Combining the last three displays proves \eqref{eq:SkL-bound}. The special cases
	\eqref{eq:SkP-bound} and \eqref{eq:SkS-bound} follow by taking $L=\Lambda$ and
	$L=\Lambda_1$, respectively.

entries.statement:

entries.name: lem:shifted-Yukawa-sums
entries.proof:
Since $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}(k)|^2=\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}$ and $2\beta>3$, the kernel $|\rmk{\widehat v_\beta}|^2$ belongs to $\ell^1(\mathbb Z^2)$.
	
	For \eqref{eq:shifted-v-sum-1}, assume first $|\ell|\ge1$ and split
	\[
	\mathbb Z^2=R_0\cup R_-\cup R_\infty,
	\qquad
	R_0:=\{|k|\le |\ell|/2\},
	\qquad
	R_-:=\{|k+\ell|\le |\ell|/2\},
	\]
	with $R_\infty$ the complement. On $R_0$ one has $|k+\ell|\ge |\ell|/2$, hence
	\[
	\sum_{k\in R_0}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s}
	\le C_{\beta,s}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s}.
	\]
	On $R_-$ write $k=-\ell+j$ with $|j|\le |\ell|/2$. Then $|k|\ge |\ell|/2$, so
	\[
	\sum_{k\in R_-}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s}
	\le C_\beta\langle \ell\rangle^{-2\beta}\sum_{|j|\le |\ell|/2}\langle j\rangle^{-2s}
	\le C_{\beta,s}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s}.
	\]
	On $R_\infty$ one has both $|k|\gtrsim |\ell|$ and $|k+\ell|\gtrsim |\ell|$, hence
	\[
	\sum_{k\in R_\infty}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s}
	\le C_{\beta,s}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s}\sum_{|k|\gtrsim |\ell|}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}
	\le C_{\beta,s}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s}.
	\]
	This proves \eqref{eq:shifted-v-sum-1} for $|\ell|\ge1$, and the case $|\ell|<1$ is immediate after enlarging the constant.
	Furthermore,  \eqref{eq:shifted-v-sum-2} follows form \eqref{eq:shifted-v-sum-1}.

%	\[
%	\mathbb Z^2=R_0\cup R_+\cup R_-\cup R_\infty,
%	\]
%	where
%	\[
%	R_0:=\{|k|\le |\ell|/2\},
%	\qquad
%	R_+:=\{|k-\ell|\le |\ell|/2\},

%	R_-:=\{|k+\ell|\le |\ell|/2\}.
%	\]

%	\sum_{k\in R_0}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s_1}\langle \ell-k\rangle^{-2s_2}
%	\le C_{\beta,s_1,s_2}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s_1}.
%	\]
%	On $R_+$ write $k=\ell+j$ with $|j|\le |\ell|/2$. Then $|k|\ge |\ell|/2$ and $|k+\ell|\gtrsim |\ell|$, hence
%	\[
%	\sum_{k\in R_+}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s_1}\langle \ell-k\rangle^{-2s_2}
%	\le C_{\beta,s_1,s_2}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2\beta-2s_1}\sum_{|j|\le |\ell|/2}\langle j\rangle^{-2s_2}
%	\le C_{\beta,s_1,s_2}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s_1}.
%	\]
%	The region $R_-$ is symmetric. Finally, on $R_\infty$ one has $|k|\gtrsim |\ell|$, $|k+\ell|\gtrsim |\ell|$, and $|\ell-k|\gtrsim |\ell|$, so
%	\[
%	\sum_{k\in R_\infty}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}\langle k+\ell\rangle^{-2s_1}\langle \ell-k\rangle^{-2s_2}
%	\le C_{\beta,s_1,s_2}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s_1}\sum_{|k|\gtrsim |\ell|}\langle k\rangle^{-2\beta}
%	\le C_{\beta,s_1,s_2}\langle \ell\rangle^{-2s_1}.
%	\]
%	This proves \eqref{eq:shifted-v-sum-2} for $|\ell|\ge1$, and the case $|\ell|<1$ is again trivial.

entries.name: proof_139
entries.statement:
[Proof of Lemma \ref{lem:free-tail-Q1}]
	By definition,
	\[
	\lambda N_{0,Q_1}
	=
	\lambda\sum_{h(p)>\Lambda_1^2}\frac{1}{e^{\lambda h(p)}-1}.
	\]
	Using the standard lattice-to-integral comparison in dimension two, we obtain
	\[
	\lambda N_{0,Q_1}
	\lesssim
	\int_{\Lambda_1^2}^{\infty}\frac{\lambda\,\,\mathrm d r}{e^{c\lambda r}-1}.
	\]
	With the change of variables $x=\lambda r$, this becomes
	\[
	\lambda N_{0,Q_1}
	\lesssim
	\int_{\lambda\Lambda_1^2}^{\infty}\frac{\,\mathrm d x}{e^{cx}-1}
	=
	\frac1c\log\frac{1}{1-e^{-c\lambda\Lambda_1^2}}.
	\]
	This proves the first claim.
	
	Now since ${\alpha_1}>1/2$, we have
	\[
	\lambda\Lambda_1^2=\lambda^{1-2{\alpha_1}}\to\infty.
	\]
	Hence, for all sufficiently small $\lambda$,
	\[
	e^{-c\lambda\Lambda_1^2}\le \frac12,
	\]
	and therefore
	\[
	\log\frac{1}{1-e^{-c\lambda\Lambda_1^2}}
	\le
	2e^{-c\lambda\Lambda_1^2}.
	\]
	Thus
	\[
	\lambda N_{0,Q_1}\le C e^{-c\lambda\Lambda_1^2}.
	\]
	Since exponential decay dominates every power of $|\log\lambda|$, we conclude that
	\[
	(1+|\log\lambda|)^m\,\lambda N_{0,Q_1}\to 0
	\qquad\text{for every }m\ge 0.
	\]
